Trust Relationship.title: W1: The Trust Relationship & Three Certainties
Trust Relationship.content:
Trusts Law – W1: The Trust Relationship

Topic Overview

Logical exam order:

1. Define a trust: equitable duty relating to property; two components (property + obligation)

2. Split of ownership: legal interest (trustee) vs equitable/beneficial interest (beneficiary)

3. Comparisons: trust vs contract, debt, charge, agency, bailment, company, estate administration

4. Benefits of trusts: 8 key benefits (separation, expertise, protection, flexibility, control, ring-fencing, tracing, tax)

5. Categories: express / resulting / constructive; testamentary / inter vivos; fixed / discretionary; charitable purpose trusts; bare trusts

6. Creation of express trusts: self-declaration vs transfer on trust

7. Three certainties: intention, subject matter, objects

8. Certainty of intention: key cases – Lyell v Kennedy; Paul v Constance; Re Kayford; Re Lehman Brothers; Modelboard; Re Oldfield; Mussoorie Bank

9. Certainty of subject matter: trust property requirement + beneficial entitlement requirement; tangible vs intangible; Hunter v Moss

10. Quistclose trusts: debt + trust combined; Barclays v Quistclose; Twinsectra; Farepak

Summary Revision Tables

Table 1: Key Definitions

Term / Concept

Definition / Rule

Trust

An equitable duty relating to property; mechanism for dividing ownership and management of property between two parties.

Trust Property

An essential and fundamental requirement of a trust; must be identifiable (Lord Browne-Wilkinson). A trust ceases if property is destroyed through no fault of the trustee.

Legal Interest

Held by the trustee; at common law the trustee is the owner and has all rights of legal ownership.

Equitable / Beneficial Interest

Held by the beneficiary; a recognised proprietary interest in equity. Can be given away or sold by the beneficiary. Beneficiary is the "true owner" in effect.

Chattel

Tangible personal property other than land (Lord Shaw in Lord Strathcona).

Chose in action

An intangible right such as a debt or company share. Can be held on trust.

Express Trust

A trust deliberately created in response to a person's intention.

Resulting Trust

Arises by operation of law where equity recognises the transferor should retain or regain the beneficial interest. Automatic or presumed.

Constructive Trust

Arises to correct unconscionability; imposed automatically on a qualifying event (institutional), or as a remedy following proprietary estoppel / breach of fiduciary duty.

Fixed Trust

Trustee has no discretion; must distribute exactly as directed by the settlor.

Discretionary Trust

Trustee has distributive discretion; settlor determines potential beneficiaries (objects), trustees determine who benefits and in what shares.

Bare Trust

Trustee holds legal title for sole beneficiary with vested interest; no discretion, no active management duties; must follow beneficiary's instructions.

Power of Appointment

A right to choose who, from a specified class of objects, receives property. Donor confers; donee exercises. Fiduciary (given to trustee) or personal (given to non-trustee).

Table 2: Trusts Distinguished from Similar Concepts

Concept

Key Distinction from Trust

Authority / Note

Contract

Arises from agreement between parties (common law); both parties owe obligations. Trust arises from settlor's intention alone; only trustee owes obligation.

—

Debt

Creditor has personal right to repayment; cannot compel application of specific assets. Beneficiary has equitable proprietary interest. A debt and trust can co-exist.

Foley v Hill [1848]; Barclays v Quistclose [1970]

Charge

Chargor can still use charged property. Chargee's interest limited to debt amount. Chargor has right of redemption. No right for beneficiary to "redeem" equivalent.

—

Agency

Agent can bind principal to third-party contracts; trustee acts as principal. Both may owe fiduciary duties. Agent may become trustee of property received for principal.

Fact-specific inquiry

Bailment

Only chattels; bailor retains legal title; transfer of possession only. Trust can cover any asset; settlor completely divests interest. Trustee can transfer legal ownership.

—

Company

Company has legal personality; trust does not. Shareholders have no proprietary interest in company assets. Directors owe duties to company, not shareholders directly.

—

Estate Administration

PRs must administer and distribute as quickly as practicable (ideally within 1 year). Beneficiary of estate has no equitable proprietary interest in assets—only personal right against PR.

—

Table 3: Quistclose Trusts – Key Rules and Cases

Principle

Rule

Authority

Bank as debtor, not trustee

Money deposited with a bank becomes the bank's money; bank is a debtor, not trustee.

Foley v Hill [1848]

Debt + Trust can co-exist

A Quistclose trust combines a debt and a trust. Money loaned for a specific purpose and NOT at free disposal of borrower generates a trust in favour of lender.

Barclays v Quistclose [1970]; Twinsectra v Yardley [2002]

Key requirement: not at free disposal

Must show MUTUAL intention that money can only be applied for the stated purpose and is NOT at free disposal of borrower. Merely paying for a purpose is insufficient.

Twinsectra v Yardley [2002]

Certainty of purpose

Purpose must be sufficiently certain: must be possible to determine whether any given application of the money falls within it. If uncertain, borrower holds entirely on trust for lender.

Twinsectra [2002]

Operation of Quistclose trust

(1) On advance: borrower holds on trust for lender, with power to use for specified purpose. (2) Money used for purpose: lender's equitable interest extinguished; relationship becomes debtor-creditor. (3) Misapplication: breach of trust; lender can assert equitable proprietary interest. (4) Impossible to apply: borrower must return money.

Twinsectra [2002]

Farepak – no Quistclose

Christmas savings scheme: money NOT held on Quistclose trust because it did not make sense for Farepak to set aside money in a separate account from January to November – it was at free disposal.

Re Farepak [2006]

Extension beyond loans

Quistclose trusts not restricted to loan contexts. Arise in any situation where property is transferred for a restricted purpose and is not at free disposal of the transferee.

Ali v Dinc [2020]

Table 4: Certainty of Intention – Key Cases

Case

Facts / Held

Principle

Re Oldfield [1904]

Testator expressed a "desire" that daughters should make provision for her son. Held: no trust.

"A desire carries no obligation except a moral one." Precatory words insufficient.

Lyell v Kennedy [1889]

Kennedy collected rent, paid into a separate account, told others it belonged to the heir. Held: trust for heir.

Separate bank account is "indicative of some sort of trust"; not indicative of a personal right or interest.

Paul v Constance [1977]

Dennis repeated "this money is as much yours as mine." Account used jointly for bingo winnings. Held: trust declared for Dennis and Doreen jointly.

Objective assessment; unsophisticated person need not use legal terminology. Repeated informal words can evidence intention.

Re Kayford [1975]

Mail order company opened "Customers' Trust Deposit Account" on insolvency advice. Held: trust for customers.

Trust created without the word "trust". Sufficient intention shown in substance. Separate account is good indicator but not necessary or conclusive.

Re Lehman Brothers [2009]

Clause 5.2 gave Lehman "full ownership" of post-administration receipts. Held: implied term that Lehman held post-admin receipts on trust for clients.

Trust can arise from implied contractual terms. Involuntary conversion of beneficiary to unsecured creditor inconsistent with agreement.

Modelboard v Outer Box [1993]

Contract stated defendant would hold "proceeds in trust." Held: NOT a trust – it was a charge; plaintiff was a secured creditor.

Characterisation by parties as "trust" is not conclusive. Court looks at substance.

Mussoorie Bank v Raynor [1882]

"Feeling confident she will act justly to our children." Held: no trust.

Uncertainty of subject matter / objects can have "reflex action" showing no intention to create a trust.

Table 5: Certainty of Subject Matter – Rules and Cases

Requirement

Rule

Key Authority

Trust property requirement

Must be possible to identify the trust property. Trust fails if property cannot be identified from the description.

Palmer v Simmonds [1854] – "bulk" of residuary estate; Wilkinson v North [2018] – "net assets"

Tangible assets from a larger bulk

CANNOT declare a trust of a specified number of tangible assets (fungible or non-fungible) from a larger bulk without identifying the specific items.

Re London Wine [1986]; Re Goldcorp [1995]

Intangible assets from a larger bulk

CAN declare a trust of a specified number of intangible, fungible assets from a larger bulk WITHOUT identifying the specific assets. All are identical.

Hunter v Moss [1993] (50 out of 950 shares valid); Re Harvard Securities [1997]

Reconceptualisation in Lehman

Briggs J: trust of intangibles works by creating beneficial co-ownership (fractional interest in all assets), not identification of specific assets.

Re Lehman Brothers [2009] EWHC 2914

Beneficial entitlement requirement

Must be possible to ascertain the beneficiary's interest in the trust property. Where a power to determine entitlement is given to C and C dies without exercising it, the trust fails.

Boyce v Boyce [1849]; Re Golay's Will Trusts [1965] – "reasonable income" = valid objective yardstick

Table 6: The Three Certainties – At a Glance

Certainty

Test / Requirement

Consequence of Failure

Intention

Objective intention to impose/assume duty characteristic of a trust. Assessed from words and conduct. Use of word "trust" neither necessary nor conclusive.

No trust arises; property may pass as gift or result back to transferor.

Subject Matter

(1) Trust property identifiable; (2) Beneficiary's interest in it ascertainable.

Trust fails; automatic resulting trust if property has been transferred to a trustee.

Objects

Beneficiaries (or purpose) must be identifiable. Fixed: complete list test. Discretionary: is/is not test. Power of appointment: is/is not test.

Trust fails; automatic resulting trust if property has been transferred.

Flashcard Q&A

Question

Answer

What are the two essential components of a trust?

Property component (identifiable trust property) and obligation component (trustee exercises legal rights for benefit of beneficiary).

Who holds the legal interest and who holds the equitable interest?

Trustee = legal interest; Beneficiary = equitable/beneficial interest. Beneficiary is "true owner" in effect.

Can a trustee be a beneficiary?

Yes, but they still owe duties to other beneficiaries and cannot use the trust purely for their own benefit.

What is the key indicator that a person cannot be a trustee (Re Bond Worth / Richmond Theatre)?

If the alleged trustee has the right to mix or use the assets freely for their own purposes, this is incompatible with a subsisting trust.

What is the key distinction between a trust and a debt?

Beneficiary has equitable proprietary interest in specific trust property; creditor has only a personal right to repayment and no recourse to specific assets.

What is a Quistclose trust?

Money lent for a specific purpose where mutual intention is that the money is NOT at the free disposal of the borrower. Borrower holds on trust for lender pending application for that purpose.

What was Twinsectra's key contribution to Quistclose?

Not sufficient to show money advanced for a purpose – must show MUTUAL intention money could ONLY be applied for that purpose and was not at free disposal of borrower.

What is the requisite intention for certainty of intention?

An intention to impose or assume the duty characteristic of a trust (duty to hold/apply property for benefit of beneficiary). Assessed objectively.

Can a trust be created informally (non-land)?

Yes. Most trusts except land and testamentary can be created orally, in writing or by conduct (Paul v Constance).

Can you declare a trust of 50 shares from 950 identical ordinary shares without identifying them?

Yes – Hunter v Moss. Intangible fungible assets are identical; specific identification unnecessary.

Can you declare a trust of 50 identical wine bottles from a stock of 200 without identifying them?

No – Re London Wine. Tangible assets require specific identification even if fungible.

What is the "reflex action" principle (Mussoorie Bank)?

If the alleged subject matter and beneficiaries' interests are so uncertain, this itself evidences an absence of intention to create a trust.

Common Exam Pitfalls

Pitfall

Correct Position

Assuming "trust" language = trust

Use of the word "trust" is NEITHER necessary NOR conclusive (Modelboard; Re Kayford). Courts look at substance.

Confusing precatory with mandatory words

"Desire," "hope," "wish" = precatory only (moral obligation). Imperative language needed for a trust duty (Re Oldfield).

Treating Quistclose as arising from purpose alone

Must show MUTUAL intention AND that money is NOT at free disposal. Paying money for a purpose is insufficient (Twinsectra; Farepak).

Applying London Wine rule to shares

Hunter v Moss creates an exception for intangible fungible assets. Tangible and intangible treated differently.

Confusing trustees and agents

Trustee acts as principal in transactions with third parties; agent can bind the principal. An agent who receives property for the principal may become a trustee – fact-specific.

Forgetting the "ministerial capacity" exception in knowing receipt

Receipt by an agent on behalf of a principal is not beneficial receipt and does not found a knowing receipt claim.

Confusing a trust with estate administration

A person interested in a deceased's estate does NOT have an equitable proprietary interest in the assets – only a personal right against the PR for proper administration.

Trust Relationship.title: W2: Beneficial Entitlement
Trust Relationship.content:
Trusts Law – W2: Beneficial Entitlement

Topic Overview

1. Certainty of objects – the third certainty; depends on type of trust

2. Fixed trusts: complete list test (IRC v Broadway Cottages); conceptual + evidential certainty

3. Discretionary trusts: is/is not test (McPhail v Doulton); still require conceptual certainty

4. Powers of appointment: is/is not test; no obligation to exercise

5. Vested interests (in possession vs in interest) vs contingent interests

6. Capital and income: successive interest trusts; life interest trusts

7. Sole beneficiary scenarios: absolutely entitled / future interest / contingent

8. Saunders v Vautier: adult, sound mind, vested interest → can collapse trust

9. Extension: multiple beneficiaries must all agree and all qualify (including gift-over objects)

Summary Revision Tables

Table 1: Certainty of Objects – Test by Trust Type

Trust Type

Applicable Test

Authority / Notes

Fixed trust (single beneficiary)

Beneficiary must be certain; any uncertainty = trust fails.

—

Fixed trust – equal distribution between class

COMPLETE LIST TEST: must be possible to draw up a complete list of all beneficiaries. Conceptual AND evidential certainty required.

IRC v Broadway Cottages Trust [1955]

Discretionary trust

IS/IS NOT TEST: can say with certainty whether "any given individual is or is not a member of the class." Must also survey the class appropriately.

McPhail v Doulton [1971]; Re Baden (No 2) [1973]

Power of appointment

IS/IS NOT TEST: same as discretionary. No need to identify every member of class. Donee not required to exercise the power.

—

Table 2: Conceptual Certainty – Examples

Class Description

Conceptually Certain?

Notes / Authority

"Employees" of company

YES – legal meaning; specify current/past/future for best drafting.

—

"Children" of an individual

YES – legal meaning. Adopted = legal children. Step-children NOT legal children of step-parent.

—

"Friends" of an individual

NO – not legally certain.

—

"Relatives" of an individual

YES – "descendants from a common ancestor" (Sachs and Megaw LJJ) OR "next of kin" (Stamp LJ).

Re Baden (No 2) [1973]

Table 3: Vested vs Contingent Interests

Interest Type

Definition

Key Indicators / Notes

Vested in possession

Current right to current enjoyment of the property.

Life tenant of a life interest trust (receives income now).

Vested in interest

Current unconditional right to FUTURE enjoyment of the property.

Remainderman under a life interest trust (right exists now; enjoyed after life tenant dies).

Contingent interest

Interest dependent on occurrence of an uncertain future event. No entitlement unless and until condition satisfied.

Look for "if": "to my son IF he survives me." Presence of gift-over (non-essential). On failure → resulting trust.

Table 4: Types of Beneficial Interest Scenario

Scenario

Rules on Capital and Income

Notes

Sole beneficiary, absolutely entitled

Adult: entitled to receive income as it arises; trustee must pay it. Minor: not entitled to receive income as it arises; trustees may apply it under power of maintenance.

—

Sole beneficiary, future (vested) interest

Interest is vested in interest now; vests in possession at specified age/time. Capital interest carries the "intermediate income." Over 18: entitled to income as it arises. Under 18: depends on trustees' powers.

"Intermediate income" = income arising during the waiting period.

Sole beneficiary, contingent interest

Nobody else has a separate interest in income, so capital carries intermediate income. Over 18: entitled to income as it arises. Under 18: accumulated.

—

Successive interest trust (life interest)

Income beneficiary (life tenant) receives income during lifetime. Capital beneficiary (remainderman) receives capital on life tenant's death.

Trustee must balance interests of life tenant (income) and remainderman (capital). Duty of even-handedness.

Table 5: Fixed vs Discretionary Trusts – Key Distinctions

Feature

Fixed Trust

Discretionary Trust

Trustee discretion

None – must distribute as directed by settlor.

Has distributive discretion; determines who benefits and in what shares.

Nature of objects' interest

Proprietary equitable interest in trust property.

No equitable interest until discretion exercised in their favour; only potential beneficiaries.

Objects' rights

Can compel proper administration; sue for breach of trust; right to be informed once interest vests.

Can sue to ensure discretion is exercised (but not in their favour specifically); can sue for breach.

Certainty of objects test

Complete list test.

Is/is not test.

Table 6: Powers of Appointment – Key Features

Feature

Rule

Distinction from Discretionary Trust

Obligation to exercise

NO obligation to exercise a power of appointment.

Discretionary trustee MUST exercise discretion within reasonable time.

Wording indicator

Permissive language ("may") = power. Imperative language ("must") = discretionary trust.

—

Gift-over

Presence of gift-over indicates a power of appointment; absence not determinative.

—

Fiduciary vs Personal power

Fiduciary (given to trustee): must periodically consider whether to exercise. Personal (given to non-trustee): no obligation to consider.

—

Rights of objects of power

Cannot compel exercise; can only constrain improper exercise.

Objects of discretionary trust have broader enforcement rights.

Table 7: Saunders v Vautier – Requirements and Extensions

Scenario

Requirements

Key Notes

Sole adult beneficiary

(1) Adult (18+); (2) Sound mind; (3) VESTED interest in the trust property.

Can direct trustee to transfer legal title; trust ends. Only where absolutely entitled.

Multiple beneficiaries – severable fixed trust

Each beneficiary separately satisfies S v V conditions; interests must be severable.

Can exercise independently if interests are severable.

Successive interest / non-severable fixed trust

ALL beneficiaries must agree; ALL must be adult and of sound mind.

All must act together; cannot be done individually.

Contingent or discretionary trust

ALL persons potentially entitled (including gift-over objects) must agree and qualify.

No single beneficiary can collapse alone – no vested interest. Very difficult in large discretionary trusts.

Legal limitations

Cannot interfere with administration while trust subsists. Choice: exercise S v V OR remain object and allow trustees to act per trust terms.

Cannot tell trustee how to perform their role. Must vary terms by exercising S v V and creating a new trust.

Flashcard Q&A

Question

Answer

What test applies to fixed trusts for certainty of objects?

Complete list test (IRC v Broadway Cottages): must be possible to draw up a complete list of ALL beneficiaries.

What test applies to discretionary trusts and powers of appointment?

Is/is not test (McPhail v Doulton): can say with certainty whether any given individual IS or IS NOT a member of the class.

What are conceptual certainty and evidential certainty?

Conceptual: precision of the language defining the class. Evidential: ability of trustees to identify objects on the evidence available.

Are "friends" conceptually certain?

No – "friends" has no legal meaning and is therefore conceptually uncertain.

Are "relatives" conceptually certain?

Yes – per Re Baden (No 2): "descendants from a common ancestor" (Sachs/Megaw) or "next of kin" (Stamp LJ).

What is the difference between "vested in possession" and "vested in interest"?

In possession = current right to current enjoyment. In interest = current right to FUTURE enjoyment (unconditional but deferred).

What is intermediate income?

Income produced by a trust between its creation and the beneficiary's interest vesting in possession. Carried by the capital interest where no separate income beneficiary.

What are the three requirements for Saunders v Vautier?

(1) Adult; (2) Sound mind; (3) Vested interest in the trust property (absolutely entitled).

Can a beneficiary with a contingent interest exercise Saunders v Vautier alone?

No. Must act together with ALL other persons potentially entitled, including gift-over objects.

What is the key indicator of a contingent (vs vested) interest?

Look for "if" / conditional language. Presence of a gift-over is helpful but not essential.

Common Exam Pitfalls

Pitfall

Correct Position

Applying the is/is not test to fixed trusts

Fixed trusts require the COMPLETE LIST TEST – must identify all beneficiaries, not just determine if any given person is/is not in the class.

Treating discretionary objects as having proprietary rights

Objects of a discretionary trust have NO equitable interest in the trust property until discretion is exercised in their favour. They are only POTENTIAL beneficiaries.

Confusing powers of appointment with discretionary trusts

Key distinction: no obligation to exercise a power; must exercise a discretionary trust. Permissive ("may") = power; imperative ("must") = discretionary trust.

Allowing a single contingent beneficiary to exercise S v V

A beneficiary with a contingent interest CANNOT exercise S v V alone. Must act together with ALL persons potentially interested, including any gift-over objects.

Forgetting to check for vesting of interest before applying S v V

S v V only applies where the beneficiary is absolutely entitled. Check: no conditions outstanding; no other person could obtain a beneficial interest; no outstanding power of appointment.

Ignoring evidential certainty

Even if the class is conceptually certain, the trustee must be able to identify members evidentially (Re Baden No 2 – claimant must prove to trustee's satisfaction they are within the class).

Trustees.title: W3: Trustees and Their Duties
Trustees.content:
Trusts Law – W3: Trustees and their Duties

Topic Overview

1. Role of trustee: voluntary, usually unpaid; joint office; must act unanimously; active role required

2. Appointment of trustees: inter vivos (self-declaration / transfer on trust); testamentary trusts; subsisting trusts

3. Statutory powers to appoint: s 36 TA 1925 (replacement/additional); s 19 TLATA (beneficiaries); s 41 TA 1925 (court)

4. Administrative powers (s 3 TA 2000 – general power of investment; s 4 – standard investment criteria; s 5 – advice; s 8 – land; s 11 – delegation)

5. Statutory duty of care (s 1 TA 2000): reasonable care and skill; higher standard for professionals

6. Breach of trust: acting outside powers OR failing to comply with duties

7. Loss assessment: falsification (misapplication) vs surcharging (other breaches); Target Holdings / AIB Group

8. Defences: exemption clauses (Armitage v Nurse); consent / instigation / acquiescence; limitation (s 31 LA 1980); laches; s 61 TA 1925

9. Apportionment: CLCA 1978; full indemnity (Re Partington; Head v Gould)

10. Protection: ouster clauses; exemption clauses; trustee insurance; Benjamin orders; s 27 TA 1925 notices; s 63 TA 1925 payment into court

Summary Revision Tables

Table 1: Appointment of Trustees – Powers and Sources

Power / Mechanism

Rules

Statute / Authority

Express power to appoint

Trust instrument may contain an express power to appoint (and remove) trustees. Settlor can reserve this for themselves.

Trust instrument

Replacement of existing trustees (s 36(1) TA 1925)

Power lies with: named person in will/trust; if none, continuing trustees or PR of last survivor. Exercisable: on death, absence abroad >1yr, minority/incapacity, retirement, refusal, unfitness. Must be in writing.

s 36(1) TA 1925

Additional trustees (s 36(6) TA 1925)

Power lies with: named person; if none, current trustees. Cannot exceed 4 (unless expressly permitted). Must be in writing.

s 36(6) TA 1925

Appointment by beneficiaries (s 19 TLATA)

All beneficiaries with S v V rights can direct trustees to appoint a new trustee. Must be in writing. Cannot be used where trust instrument contains express power to appoint.

s 19 TLATA 1996

Appointment by court (s 41 TA 1925)

Court of last resort. Applies where no one is authorised, able or willing to appoint. Court considers settlor's wishes, beneficiary disputes, effect on administration. Charity Commission can appoint for charities.

s 41 TA 1925; s 69(1)(b) Charities Act 2011

Disclaiming trustee (s 36(8) TA 1925)

Trustee who declines role treated as "refusing" trustee; can appoint own replacement if willing. If not, PR or court steps in.

s 36(8) TA 1925

Table 2: Administrative Powers and Duties (TA 2000)

Power / Duty

Content

Statute

General power of investment (s 3)

Trustee can make any kind of investment as if absolutely entitled to assets. Subject to standard investment criteria and advice requirements.

s 3 TA 2000

Standard investment criteria (s 4)

Must consider: (1) SUITABILITY – general suitability of the type AND specific suitability of the particular investment; (2) DIVERSIFICATION – need to diversify the portfolio. Must review investments regularly. Balances preservation of assets vs income/capital growth (duty of even-handedness).

s 4 TA 2000; Cowan v Scargill [1985]

Obligation to take advice (s 5)

Must obtain and consider "proper advice" before exercising investment powers and when reviewing. Advice from person with ability and practical experience in financial matters. May dispense with if reasonably concludes unnecessary (eg cost outweighs benefit; trustee has sufficient expertise).

s 5 TA 2000

Acquisition of land (s 8)

Statutory power to acquire freehold or leasehold UK land (not overseas). For investment OR occupation of beneficiary. When for investment: ss 4 and 5 apply. Statutory duty of care applies to all land acquisition.

s 8 TA 2000

Delegation (s 11)

Broad power to delegate investment and land acquisition powers. Cannot delegate distributive obligations. Cannot delegate to a beneficiary (even if also trustee) (s 12). Must be by written agreement with policy statement (s 15). Trustees must select appropriate agent, review regularly. If duties met, no vicarious liability for agent's negligence.

ss 11-15 TA 2000

Table 3: Statutory Duty of Care (s 1 TA 2000)

Category

Standard

Notes

Lay trustee

Exercise such care and skill as is reasonable in the circumstances, having regard to any special knowledge or experience the trustee has OR holds themselves out as having.

Only applies to acts listed in Sch 1 TA 2000.

Professional trustee

Higher standard – assessed on the special knowledge or experience that is reasonable to expect of a person acting in that PROFESSIONAL capacity.

Higher standard regardless of individual expertise.

Common law duty of care

Exercise of diligence and care expected of an ordinary prudent businessperson.

Separate from statutory duty.

Cowan v Scargill [1985]

Obligation to act in BEST FINANCIAL interest of beneficiaries. Personal views of trustee irrelevant. Cannot refuse investments on principled grounds if it reduces fund. Trustees not bound by advice but cannot ignore it without reasonable justification.

Key principles for investment standard.

Table 4: Breach of Trust – Liability and Loss Assessment

Issue

Rule

Authority

What constitutes breach

Acting outside powers (misapplication/misappropriation of trust funds; unauthorised investments) OR complying with powers but breaching associated duties (failure to consider investment criteria; failure to monitor; failure to act in beneficiaries' best interests).

—

Co-trustee liability

Co-trustees are jointly and severally liable. All must take an active role. Trustees are only liable for their OWN breaches but failure to be active = own breach.

—

Pre-appointment breach

Trustee not liable for breach before appointment. On appointment, must commence proceedings against former trustee; failure = own breach.

Re Strahan [1856]

Post-retirement liability

Still liable for breaches during trusteeship. Only liable for post-retirement breaches if retired to FACILITATE the breach OR parted with trust property without due regard (Head v Gould).

Head v Gould [1898]

Falsification (misapplication)

Court "falsifies" account; trustees must reconstitute fund to position it would have been in but for the breach. Trustees pay equitable compensation if same property cannot be restored.

Pre-Target Holdings traditional approach; still applies to traditional trusts

Surcharging (other breaches)

Court assesses expected value of trust if breach not occurred. Trustees pay equitable compensation for loss of which breach is "but for" cause (reparation claim). Assessed against what prudent trustees would have done.

—

Bare commercial trusts

Target Holdings approach: "but for" causation only; no obligation to reconstitute fund once underlying commercial transaction completed. Endorsed by SC.

Target Holdings v Redferns [1996]; AIB Group v Redler [2014]

Offsetting losses against gains

Generally CANNOT offset losses on one investment against gains on another. Can offset where arising from the SAME transaction or course of dealing.

Bartlett v Barclays Bank [1980]

Table 5: Defences to Breach of Trust

Defence

Rules

Authority

Exemption clause

Can exclude liability for any breach EXCEPT fraudulent. Does not protect against dishonest breaches.

Armitage v Nurse [1998]

Fully informed consent

Trustees not liable if received fully informed consent of ALL beneficiaries. Where only some consent: partial defence against those beneficiaries only. Subsequent affirmation = acquiescence.

—

Impounding beneficiary's interest

Where beneficiary instigates breach: court can impound their interest to indemnify trustees. Statutory power (s 62 TA 1925): no requirement for beneficiary to benefit from breach; consent need not be in writing. Common law: must benefit from breach; consent not required in writing.

s 62 TA 1925

Statutory limitation (s 21 LA 1980)

6 years from breach for claims by beneficiaries with interests vested in possession. Future interest: runs from when interest vests in possession. Does NOT apply to fraudulent breaches or proprietary claims.

s 21(3) LA 1980

Laches (equitable)

Even within limitation period, trustees can argue beneficiary knew of breach but delayed unacceptably, making it unconscionable to assert beneficial interest. Fact-specific.

—

Section 61 TA 1925

Court may excuse trustee if acted (1) honestly, (2) reasonably, AND (3) ought fairly to be excused. All three elements required. Court has wide discretion; not used lightly. Easier for lay trustees; helpful if legal advice sought first (Re Evans).

s 61 TA 1925; Re Evans [1999]

Table 6: Protection of Trustees During Administration

Mechanism

How It Works

Notes / Cases

Legal advice on interpretation

Trustees can seek legal advice and act on it; does not automatically prevent liability if wrong. Lawyer should advise on certainty and further steps.

Not a guaranteed defence

Seeking court directions

Safest option. Trustees acting on court directions will never be liable even if subsequent claim. Expensive.

—

s 48 AJA 1985

Step 1: obtain written legal opinion from barrister or solicitor with 10+ years' experience. Step 2: apply to High Court for authorisation to rely on it. HC grants order without argument unless there is a dispute.

s 71 Courts and Legal Services Act 1990

Surrendering discretion to court

Where trustees are in disagreement, court makes the decision. Only available for a specific problem – cannot abdicate ongoing administration.

—

Benjamin orders (missing beneficiaries)

Court permits distribution on a stated assumption (usually that missing beneficiary is dead). Requires full enquiries. Relieves personal liability; does not defeat claims against recipients.

Re Benjamin [1902]; Re Green's Will Trusts [1985]

s 27 TA 1925 notice (unknown beneficiaries)

Publish notice of intention to distribute (London Gazette + appropriate newspapers). Two months' notice. After two months: can distribute to known beneficiaries without personal liability to unknowns.

s 27 TA 1925

Payment into court (s 63 TA 1925)

Where genuine doubt as to location of beneficiaries and all realistic options have failed. Court gets legal control; trustees effectively retire.

s 63 TA 1925; last resort

Table 7: Apportionment of Liability – CLCA 1978 and Indemnity

Scenario

Rule

Authority

General contribution

Court can require just and equitable contribution from co-trustees under s 1-2 CLCA 1978. Presumes equal responsibility but can depart based on culpability/expertise differences.

CLCA 1978

Full indemnity

Court may award full indemnity where: (1) particular trustee is morally culpable; (2) trustee is also a beneficiary; (3) trustee-solicitor whose breach was committed in reliance on their advice.

Re Partington [1857]; Head v Gould [1898]

Re Partington – solicitor trustee

Solicitor took sole responsibility, failed to inform widow co-trustee, made unauthorised investment. Full indemnity for widow: reasonable to rely on solicitor's advice.

Re Partington [1857]

Head v Gould – no indemnity

Lay trustee took an active role in the breach. No controlling influence of solicitor trustee. Indemnity inappropriate.

Head v Gould [1898]

Flashcard Q&A

Question

Answer

What is the irreducible core of trusteeship?

A general duty to act honestly and in good faith for the benefit of the beneficiaries. Cannot be removed; must exist for a trust to be enforceable.

Who can appoint replacement trustees under s 36(1) TA 1925?

Named person in will/trust deed; if none, the continuing trustees; or the PR of the last surviving trustee.

What are the standard investment criteria under s 4 TA 2000?

(1) Suitability (general and specific); (2) Need for diversification. Must consider both when making and reviewing investments.

What does s 5 TA 2000 require?

Trustees must obtain and consider proper advice before exercising investment powers and when reviewing investments. May dispense if reasonably concludes unnecessary.

What acts are covered by the statutory duty of care (s 1 TA 2000)?

Acts listed in Schedule 1 TA 2000, including: exercising powers of investment; acquiring land; delegating; acting in relation to nominees and custodians.

Can a trustee offset losses on one investment against gains on another?

Generally NO. Exception: where losses and gains arise from the SAME transaction or course of dealing (Bartlett v Barclays Bank).

What is the distinction between falsification and surcharging?

Falsification: misapplication – restore trust to pre-breach position. Surcharging: other breaches – equitable compensation for "but for" loss assessed against what prudent trustees would have done.

What did Target Holdings establish?

For bare commercial trusts (only subsisting during an underlying commercial transaction), "but for" causation applies; trustee has no ongoing obligation to reconstitute the fund once the transaction is complete.

What are the requirements for s 61 TA 1925 relief?

Must show: (1) acted honestly; (2) acted reasonably; (3) ought fairly to be excused. All three required; court has wide discretion.

What is the effect of Armitage v Nurse on exemption clauses?

An exemption clause can exclude liability for any breach of trust EXCEPT fraudulent breach. A trustee who acts dishonestly cannot rely on an exemption clause.

What is a Benjamin order?

A court order permitting trustees to distribute on the basis of a stated assumption (typically that a missing beneficiary is presumed dead) after full enquiries have failed to locate them.

What is the effect of a s 27 TA 1925 notice?

After two months from publishing notice in London Gazette and appropriate newspapers, trustees can distribute to known beneficiaries without personal liability to unknown beneficiaries who subsequently emerge.

Common Exam Pitfalls

Pitfall

Correct Position

Thinking all breaches use "but for" causation after Target Holdings

Target Holdings only applies to BARE COMMERCIAL trusts. For traditional express trusts, the traditional falsification/surcharging approach still applies.

Forgetting that professionals are held to a higher standard

s 1(1)(b) TA 2000: professional trustees are assessed on the special knowledge and experience reasonably expected of a person in that professional capacity – a higher bar than lay trustees.

Allowing trustees to offset unrelated gains against losses

Cannot set off profits from other investments against a loss from a breach. Offset only possible where arising from the SAME transaction (Bartlett).

Misapplying s 61 TA 1925 too easily

All three elements (honesty + reasonableness + fairness to excuse) are required. The court uses s 61 sparingly; denying a remedy to beneficiaries is a serious step.

Thinking consent is a complete defence where only some beneficiaries consent

Only a PARTIAL defence – operates against those beneficiaries who consented. Non-consenting beneficiaries can still claim.

Treating personal liability and impounding as equivalent

Impounding reduces or eliminates the instigating beneficiary's own share. It is a reduction of their interest, not a claim against them personally.

Fiduciaries.title: W4: Fiduciaries and Accessory Liability
Fiduciaries.content:
Trusts Law – W4: Fiduciaries and Accessory Liability

Topic Overview

1. Fiduciary relationships: single-minded loyalty; two core duties: no-conflict and no-profit

2. Accessory liability (dishonest assistance): four requirements (Group Seven v Nasir); fault = dishonesty

3. Dishonesty test: Ivey v Genting – (1) ascertain subjective knowledge/belief; (2) apply objective standard of ordinary decent people

4. Assistance: planning, committing, or covering up breach; must be more than minimal; procuring/instigating also sufficient

5. Remedies for dishonest assistance: loss caused by breach; also profits (Novoship – "real or effective" cause; court has discretion)

6. Knowing receipt (recipient liability): four requirements (El Ajou); fault = knowledge making retention unconscionable

7. Beneficial receipt requirement: must be in own right (Trustor v Smallbone); ministerial receipt insufficient (Agip)

8. Persistence of equitable proprietary interest requirement: Byers v Saudi National Bank

9. Knowledge test: BCCI v Akindele – unconscionability; Baden scale types 1–5; Armstrong DLW applied

10. Claims can be run in the alternative; different remedies: dishonest assistance may yield greater remedy

Summary Revision Tables

Table 1: Fiduciary Duties – Core Rules

Duty

Content and Scope

Fiduciary relationship

One party owes a duty of single-minded loyalty to the other. Arises in established categories (trustee-beneficiary, solicitor-client, director-company, agent-principal) and can arise on facts.

No-conflict rule

A fiduciary must not put themselves in a position where their personal interests conflict with their duties to the principal. Applies regardless of whether any harm results to the principal.

No-profit rule

A fiduciary must not obtain an unauthorised benefit (for themselves or a third party) as a result of their position. Applies even where the profit does not harm the principal.

Breach without loss

A trustee may breach fiduciary duties even if the trust has not suffered a financial loss — e.g. making an authorised, profitable investment while secretly taking a commission (unauthorised personal profit).

Table 2: Accessory Liability (Dishonest Assistance) – Requirements

Element

Rule

Authority

(1) Trust in existence

Must be a trust (or fiduciary duty) in existence at the material time.

Group Seven Ltd v Nasir [2019]

(2) Breach by trustee/fiduciary

The trustee or fiduciary must have committed a breach of that trust or fiduciary duty.

Group Seven [2019]

(3) Assistance by defendant

D must assist in planning, committing, or covering up the breach. Must be MORE than minimal and must have made commission/concealment easier. Also sufficient: D procures or instigates the breach (Eaves v Hickson). D cannot avoid liability by showing breach would have occurred without their help.

Group Seven [2019]; Ultraframe v Fielding [2005]; Eaves v Hickson [1861]

(4) D's assistance was dishonest

Dishonesty is required (not mere negligence). Two-stage test: (1) ascertain D's actual knowledge/belief as to the facts (SUBJECTIVE); (2) apply the objective standard of ordinary decent people to those facts (OBJECTIVE). Dishonesty is not a requirement for knowing receipt.

Royal Brunei Airlines v Tan [1995]; Ivey v Genting Casinos [2017]

Table 3: Accessory Liability – Key Cases

Case

Facts / Held

Principle

Royal Brunei Airlines v Tan [1995]

Airline's ticket agent held proceeds on trust; MD caused company to misapply them. Company went insolvent. MD held personally liable.

Established dishonesty as the fault requirement for accessory liability. Trustee need not be dishonest.

Ivey v Genting Casinos [2017]

SC reformulated dishonesty test. Two-stage: (1) subjective knowledge of facts; (2) objective assessment of conduct against standard of ordinary decent people.

Replaces prior Ghosh test for civil and criminal dishonesty.

Starglade Properties v Nash [2010]

Nash caused company to misapply trust property dishonestly. Held liable. Irrelevant that Nash did not know a trust existed.

Knowledge of the existence of a trust is NOT a prerequisite for liability.

Group Seven v Nasir [2019]

Reformulated requirements. On causation: must show D's conduct assisted the breach AND that but for the breach the loss would not have occurred.

Leading authority on requirements and causation for dishonest assistance.

Novoship (UK) v Mikhaylyuk [2014]

Dishonest assistant also potentially liable for profits acquired by reason of participation in breach. Must show participation was the "real or effective" cause. "But for" insufficient. Court has discretion; remedy withheld if disproportionate.

Extends remedy to account of profits in appropriate cases.

Table 4: Knowing Receipt (Recipient Liability) – Requirements

Element

Rule

Authority

(1) Misapplication of trust property

Misapplication of trust property or property held in another fiduciary capacity.

El Ajou v Dollar Land Holdings [1994]

(2) Beneficial receipt

D must receive the property "for their own benefit or in their own right in the sense of setting up a title of their own." Receipt in a MINISTERIAL capacity is INSUFFICIENT (e.g. agent receiving for principal).

Trustor AB v Smallbone (No 2) [2001]; Agip (Africa) v Jackson [1990]

Bank receipt – in credit

Where money is paid to a bank and credited to an IN CREDIT account: bank does NOT receive beneficially (creates debtor-creditor relationship).

Agip (Africa) v Jackson [1990]; Polly Peck v Nadir (No 2) [1992]

Bank receipt – overdrawn

Where money is paid to a bank and credited to an OVERDRAWN account: bank DOES receive beneficially to the extent the payment reduces/discharges indebtedness.

Uzinterimpex JSC v Standard Bank [2008]

(3) Persistence of equitable proprietary interest

If the transfer to D extinguishes the claimant's equitable proprietary interest, no knowing receipt claim lies. Example: overreaching under s 2 LPA 1925 extinguishes beneficiaries' interest when land sold to purchaser paying purchase money to two trustees.

Byers v Saudi National Bank [2022]

(4) Knowledge making retention unconscionable

Recipient's state of knowledge must make it unconscionable to retain the benefit. Dishonesty NOT required. If recipient disposes/dissipates before acquiring knowledge: no personal liability. Once knowledge acquired: must restore or face personal liability.

BCCI v Akindele [2001]; Re Montagu's Settlement [1987]

Table 5: Baden Scale of Knowledge

Type

Description

Effect (Armstrong DLW v Winnington [2012])

1

Actual knowledge.

Renders receipt unconscionable. D is liable in knowing receipt.

2

Wilfully shutting one's eyes to the obvious.

Renders receipt unconscionable. D is liable in knowing receipt.

3

Wilfully and recklessly failing to make enquiries that an honest and reasonable person would make.

Renders receipt unconscionable. D is liable in knowing receipt.

4

Knowledge of circumstances which would indicate the facts to an honest and reasonable person.

Only renders receipt unconscionable if a reasonable person would have (a) appreciated the transfer was probably in breach of trust AND (b) made enquiries or sought advice which would have revealed the probability.

5

Knowledge of circumstances which would put an honest and reasonable person on inquiry.

Same as Type 4 – higher threshold; must also show the reasonable person would have followed through with enquiries.

Table 6: Dishonest Assistance vs Knowing Receipt – Comparison

Feature

Dishonest Assistance

Knowing Receipt

Fault requirement

DISHONESTY (Ivey v Genting two-stage test).

KNOWLEDGE making retention unconscionable (BCCI v Akindele). Dishonesty NOT required.

Need to receive property?

NO – liability based on assistance, not receipt.

YES – must beneficially receive the misapplied property or traceable proceeds.

Remedy

Personal liability for the LOSS CAUSED by the breach (regardless of whether D personally profited). Plus possible account of profits (Novoship – "real or effective" cause; court's discretion).

Personal liability limited to the VALUE of what was BENEFICIALLY RECEIVED. Plus possible proprietary claim if D still holds the property.

Basis of liability

Secondary/accessory liability. D assists primary wrongdoer.

Primary liability. D has received trust property and retained it unconscionably.

Can claims be combined?

Yes – both claims can be pleaded in the alternative against the same defendant.

Yes – same defendant may face both claims; different requirements mean one may succeed where other fails.

Liability Structures

The following structures set out the analytical steps for each potential claim. Work through each stage in order when answering a problem question.

Structure 1: Fiduciary Duty / Breach of Fiduciary Duty

1

Is there a fiduciary relationship?

Identify a recognised fiduciary relationship (e.g. trustee-beneficiary, solicitor-client, director-company, agent-principal) or establish one on the facts. The core obligation is single-minded loyalty.

2

Has the fiduciary breached their fiduciary duty?

Consider which duty has been breached. There are two:

(A) Unauthorised profit (no-profit rule): the fiduciary has obtained a personal benefit from their position without authorisation.

(B) No-conflict rule: the fiduciary has placed themselves in a position where their personal interest conflicts with their duty to the principal.

Note: a breach can occur even where the trust fund has not suffered a financial loss and even where the transaction was otherwise beneficial.

3

Remedy?

The remedy depends on which duty was breached:

(A) Unauthorised profit: Account of profits (personal remedy) or constructive trust over the profit (proprietary remedy). The constructive trust is preferable where the fiduciary is insolvent or the profit has appreciated in value.

(B) No-conflict rule: Equitable compensation (personal remedy) for any loss caused to the principal by the conflict.

4

Defences?

Consider whether any defences are available: fully informed consent of the beneficiaries/principal; authorisation in the trust instrument or by the court; statutory relief (s 61 TA 1925 for trustee-fiduciaries). Note: no defence available for fraudulent breach.

Structure 2: Accessory Liability (Dishonest Assistance)

1

Trust / fiduciary relationship

There must be a trust or fiduciary duty in existence at the material time (Group Seven Ltd v Nasir [2019]). Identify the trust or fiduciary relationship and confirm it existed when the defendant assisted.

2

Breach of trust / fiduciary duty

The trustee or fiduciary must have committed a breach of that trust or fiduciary duty. The trustee/fiduciary does NOT need to have acted dishonestly (Royal Brunei Airlines v Tan [1995]).

3

Assistance

The defendant must have assisted the trustee/fiduciary in planning, committing, or covering up the breach. Also sufficient to procure or instigate the breach (Eaves v Hickson [1861]). The assistance must be more than minimal and must have made commission or concealment easier (Ultraframe v Fielding [2005]; Group Seven). It is no defence that the breach would have occurred without the assistance.

4

Dishonest — two-stage test (Ivey v Genting Casinos [2017])

Stage 1 — Subjective knowledge: ascertain the defendant's actual state of knowledge or belief as to the facts (subjective inquiry).

Stage 2 — Objective standard: applying those facts, was the defendant's conduct dishonest by the standards of ordinary decent people (objective inquiry)? The defendant does not need to know that a trust legally exists — knowledge of the relevant facts is sufficient (Starglade v Nash [2010]).

5

Extent of liability

Losses: the dishonest assistant is personally liable for the full loss occasioned by the breach which they assisted. The claimant must show that D's conduct assisted the breach AND that but for the breach the loss would not have occurred (Group Seven).

Profits: D may also be liable to account for any profits they personally acquired as a result of their participation in the breach, provided their participation was the real or effective cause of the profit. The court has a discretion to grant or withhold this remedy where it would be disproportionate (Novoship v Mikhaylyuk [2014]).

Flashcard Q&A

Question

Answer

What are the two core fiduciary duties?

(1) No-conflict: must not place personal interests in conflict with duties to principal. (2) No-profit: must not obtain unauthorised benefit from their fiduciary position.

What are the four requirements for dishonest assistance?

(1) Trust in existence; (2) trustee committed a breach; (3) D assisted that breach; (4) D's assistance was dishonest. (Group Seven v Nasir)

What is the dishonesty test after Ivey v Genting?

(1) Ascertain D's ACTUAL subjective knowledge/belief as to the facts; (2) Ask whether conduct was dishonest by the objective standard of ordinary decent people.

Can a defendant escape liability by showing the breach would have occurred anyway?

No. Any assistance, however small above minimal, creates liability. D cannot avoid liability by showing the breach would have occurred without their help.

Is dishonesty required for knowing receipt?

NO. Knowing receipt requires only knowledge making it unconscionable to retain the receipt (BCCI v Akindele).

What is "ministerial receipt" and why does it not found a knowing receipt claim?

Receipt by an agent on behalf of a principal – the agent does not receive the property in their own right; they hold it for the principal. No beneficial receipt (Agip v Jackson).

When does a bank beneficially receive money paid into a customer's account?

Only when the account is OVERDRAWN – to the extent the payment reduces/discharges the customer's indebtedness. An in-credit account creates only a debtor-creditor relationship.

What did Byers v Saudi National Bank establish?

If the transfer of the misapplied property to D extinguishes the claimant's equitable proprietary interest (e.g. by overreaching), no knowing receipt claim will lie.

What is the effect of Baden Types 1–3 vs Types 4–5?

Types 1–3 automatically render receipt unconscionable. Types 4–5 only do so if a reasonable person would also have appreciated the breach was probable AND made further enquiries (Armstrong DLW).

What is the remedy for dishonest assistance vs knowing receipt?

Dishonest assistance: full loss caused by the breach (not limited to personal gain). Knowing receipt: value of what was beneficially received.

Common Exam Pitfalls

Pitfall

Correct Position

Requiring dishonesty for knowing receipt

WRONG. Knowing receipt only requires knowledge making retention unconscionable (BCCI v Akindele). Dishonesty is the requirement for DISHONEST ASSISTANCE, not knowing receipt.

Requiring the trustee to be dishonest for accessory liability

The trustee need NOT be dishonest. It is the ASSISTANT's dishonesty that matters (Royal Brunei v Tan).

Thinking D must know a trust exists for dishonest assistance

Knowledge that a trust exists is NOT a prerequisite. D need only act dishonestly in assisting the breach (Starglade v Nash).

Confusing ministerial and beneficial receipt

An agent receiving property for a principal receives ministerially – NOT beneficially. No knowing receipt claim unless agent receives in their own right.

Treating in-credit bank accounts as beneficial receipt

Bank credited to an IN CREDIT account does NOT receive beneficially – only creates a debt. Only OVERDRAWN accounts can give rise to beneficial receipt by the bank.

Mixing up the remedies for the two claims

Dishonest assistance: liability for full LOSS caused by breach (can exceed what D received). Knowing receipt: limited to VALUE BENEFICIALLY RECEIVED by D.

Tracing.title: W5: Proprietary Interests & Tracing
Tracing.content:
Trusts Law – W5: Proprietary Interest, Tracing and Equitable Remedies

Topic Overview

1. Equitable remedies: discretionary; personal (equitable compensation) vs proprietary; advantages of proprietary claims

2. Following: tracking same asset as it moves from hand to hand (Foskett)

3. Tracing: identifying a new asset as the substitute for the old (Foskett); requires series of direct substitutions (Relfo)

4. Claiming: assertion of personal or proprietary right in the misapplied property or traceable proceeds

5. Conditions for following/tracing (Re Diplock): (1) equity recognised right of property; (2) fiduciary relationship with claimant

6. Simple case (unmixed funds): beneficial ownership or equitable lien

7. Wrongful mixtures: Re Hallett (dissipations = trustee's own money); Re Oatway (identifiable asset = trust); cherry-picking (Shalson)

8. Innocent mixtures: ratable sharing (Re Diplock). Current accounts: Clayton's Case (FIFO, readily displaced); pari passu ex post facto; rolling charge method

9. Proprietary claims: unmixed → beneficial ownership or lien; wrongful mixture → proportionate share or lien (trustee subordinated); innocent mixture → proportionate share only

10. Subrogation: Boscawen v Bajwa – where trust money pays off a secured debt

11. Defence: bona fide purchaser of legal interest without notice

Summary Revision Tables

Table 1: Following, Tracing and Claiming – Definitions

Concept

Definition (Foskett v McKeown [2001])

Key Point

Following

The process of following the same asset as it moves from hand to hand.

Locates the misapplied trust property itself.

Tracing

The process of identifying a new asset as the substitute for the old. General rule: one asset is the traceable proceed of another if there is "a series of direct substitutions between them" (Relfo v Varsani [2014]).

Identifies a substitute asset when original has been exchanged.

Claiming

The assertion of a personal or proprietary right in relation to misapplied trust property or its traceable proceeds.

The remedy-seeking step after following/tracing.

Conditions (Re Diplock [1948])

(1) Claimant had a "right of property recognised by equity" in the asset; AND (2) asset held by person in a FIDUCIARY relationship with the claimant. Applied liberally: extends to executor/director misapplications.

Easily satisfied for express trusts. Diplock applied liberally.

Table 2: Benefits of Proprietary vs Personal Claims

Proprietary Claim – Advantages

Limits / Notes

Not affected by D's bankruptcy or insolvency – beneficiary ranks ahead of unsecured creditors.

Must identify a specific asset representing the proceeds of the breach.

Captures increases in value of traceable proceeds.

Cannot make proprietary claim against bona fide purchaser of legal interest without notice.

Does not depend on fault – can be maintained against innocent recipients.

Tracing rules must first be satisfied to identify the asset.

Table 3: Tracing Rules – Mixed Funds in Bank Accounts

Scenario

Rule

Simple / unmixed

Asset acquired exclusively with misapplied trust money. Beneficiary can assert beneficial ownership OR enforce equitable lien (preferred where asset has fallen in value).

Wrongful mixture – dissipations (Re Hallett [1880])

Trustee's dissipations attributed to TRUSTEE's money first. Trust money treated as remaining balance. He cannot take trust money when he has the right to take his own.

Wrongful mixture – identifiable asset later (Re Oatway [1903])

Cannot use Hallett to claim identifiable asset when trust money later dissipated. Order of withdrawals NOT determinative – identifiable asset attributed to the trust.

Cherry-picking (Shalson v Russo [2003])

In a two-party dispute (beneficiary vs trustee only): beneficiary can attribute MOST PROFITABLE applications to trust. Cannot cherry-pick where it would prejudice innocent third parties.

Innocent mixtures – general (Re Diplock)

Withdrawals attributed RATEABLY to contributors. All innocent parties share rateably; cannot prefer one over another (Foskett).

Innocent mixtures – current accounts (Clayton's Case [1816])

FIRST IN FIRST OUT (FIFO). First money in is deemed first money out. Readily displaced where contrary to parties' intentions, impracticable, or unfair (Barlow Clowes [1992]). Disapplied in every case since Barlow Clowes.

Pari passu ex post facto

Identify all amounts contributed; attribute ALL withdrawals and remaining balance fractionally to all contributors regardless of order. Single calculation after the event.

Rolling charge method (preferred)

Each individual withdrawal attributed fractionally to contributors immediately BEFORE that withdrawal. Fractional contribution recalculated every time money is credited. Generally preferred over pari passu (Barlow Clowes; Shalson).

Table 4: Proprietary Claims – Type of Claim Available by Mixture Type

Asset Type

Available Claim(s)

Notes

Unmixed – trust property followed

Assert equitable proprietary interest; holder must restore to fund (Re Diplock).

—

Unmixed – asset acquired exclusively with misapplied trust money

(1) Assert BENEFICIAL OWNERSHIP of asset; OR (2) Enforce EQUITABLE LIEN over asset to secure personal claim against trustee.

Choose lien where asset has decreased in value – converts personal claim into a secured one.

Wrongful mixture (trust + trustee's own money)

(1) Claim PROPORTIONATE SHARE of asset (captures increases in value); OR (2) Enforce EQUITABLE LIEN for amount misapplied.

Trustee's interest SUBORDINATED to beneficiary's – trustee cannot claim share until beneficiary fully satisfied.

Innocent mixture (trust money + innocent third party)

PROPORTIONATE SHARE only (Diplock; Foskett). Cannot assert lien over innocent party's share.

All innocent contributors treated equally; share rateably.

Subrogation (Boscawen v Bajwa [1996])

Where misapplied trust money pays off a secured debt, beneficiary is subrogated to creditor's security rights.

Steps into creditor's shoes; asserts equivalent security.

Table 5: Key Tracing Cases

Case

Key Rule

Scenario

Foskett v McKeown [2001]

Defines following, tracing, claiming. Innocent mixtures: share rateably. Tracing extends to physical mixtures of fungible goods.

Life insurance premiums paid with misapplied trust money; beneficiaries traced into payout.

Re Hallett's Estate [1880]

Trustee's dissipations attributed to trustee's OWN money, preserving trust money as remaining balance.

Wrongful mixture in solicitor's bank account.

Re Oatway [1903]

Cannot use Hallett to claim identifiable asset when trust money later dissipated. Order of withdrawals NOT determinative.

Trustee bought shares and later dissipated remainder.

Shalson v Russo [2003]

Beneficiary can cherry-pick most profitable applications in two-party dispute. Cannot cherry-pick where third parties prejudiced.

Wrongful mixture; various applications of mixed fund.

Barlow Clowes v Vaughn [1992]

Clayton's Case disapplied where contrary to parties' intentions, impracticable, or unfair. Disapplied in every subsequent case.

Investment fraud; multiple innocent contributors.

Re Diplock [1948]

Conditions for following/tracing; ratable sharing for innocent mixtures.

Executors paid wrong charities; estate sought remedy.

Boscawen v Bajwa [1996]

Subrogation available where trust money used to pay off a secured debt.

Mortgage paid off with misapplied funds.

Worked Examples

The following examples illustrate how the tracing rules and proprietary claims operate in practice. All figures are chosen for clarity of calculation.

Example 1 – Simple Case (Unmixed Funds)

Facts

T is the trustee of a trust fund containing £20,000.

T withdraws the entire £20,000 from the trust account and uses it to purchase shares (now worth £25,000).

T keeps no money of their own in the account — the shares are purchased EXCLUSIVELY with trust money.

The trust has been misapplied. The beneficiaries wish to make a claim.

Step 1 — Can the beneficiaries follow or trace?

Following: The £20,000 was held in the trust account under an express trust (Re Diplock conditions satisfied). The beneficiaries can follow the cash as it left the trust account.

Tracing: The shares are the direct substitute for the £20,000 — a single direct substitution (Relfo v Varsani). The shares are the traceable proceeds of the misapplied trust money.

Step 2 — What proprietary claim is available?

Because the shares were acquired EXCLUSIVELY with trust money (unmixed), the beneficiaries can choose between two claims:

Option

Outcome and When to Use It

(A) Assert beneficial ownership of the shares

Beneficiaries claim they are the true owners of the shares. Since shares have risen from £20,000 → £25,000, they capture the £5,000 increase in value. This is the better option where the asset has appreciated.

(B) Enforce an equitable lien for £20,000

Beneficiaries secure their £20,000 personal claim against the trustee over the shares as an asset. The lien makes the personal claim secured rather than unsecured — useful if T is insolvent. Better if the shares have FALLEN in value (e.g. to £15,000) — claiming ownership would crystallise a loss, but a lien secures the full £20,000.

Key point: Where the asset has INCREASED in value → assert beneficial ownership to capture the gain. Where it has DECREASED → equitable lien secures the original sum as a priority claim.

Example 2 – Wrongful Mixture: Re Hallett Principle (Dissipation)

Facts

T is trustee of a trust. T mixes £10,000 of trust money and £10,000 of T's own money in one bank account (balance: £20,000).

T then withdraws £10,000 and spends it on a holiday (dissipation — no traceable proceeds).

The remaining balance is £10,000.

T claims the remaining £10,000 is trust money that was "spent first" since it went in first.

Applying Re Hallett's Estate [1880]

T's argument fails. A trustee "cannot be heard to say that he took away the trust money when he had a right to take away his own money." The dissipation is attributed to T's OWN money, not the trust money.

Event

Trustee's £

Trust £

Balance £

Opening balance

0

0

0

T pays in £10k trust money

0

10,000

10,000

T pays in £10k own money

10,000

10,000

20,000

T withdraws £10k (holiday — dissipated)

DISSIPATION attributed here

—

10,000

Remaining balance

0

10,000

10,000

Result: The remaining £10,000 is entirely traceable to the trust. The beneficiaries can assert beneficial ownership of the £10,000 balance, OR enforce an equitable lien over it.

Example 3 – Wrongful Mixture: Re Oatway Principle (Asset + Later Dissipation)

Facts

T is trustee of a trust. T mixes £10,000 of trust money and £10,000 of T's own money in one account (balance: £20,000).

T withdraws £10,000 FIRST and uses it to buy shares (now worth £15,000).

T then withdraws the remaining £10,000 and dissipates it on a holiday.

T argues (using Hallett) that since the trust money "went in first", the shares should be attributed to the trust — so the balance (which T now claims was "T's money") is dissipated, and the shares belong to T.

Applying Re Oatway [1903]

T's argument fails. Per Oatway: identifiable asset attributed to trust; dissipation attributed to trustee's own money. Order is not determinative.

Event

T pays in £10k trust money

10,000

T withdraws £10k → buys shares

DISSIPATION attributed here

10,000 → shares

10,000

T withdraws remaining £10k (holiday)

Result

—

Shares = traceable trust asset

—

Result: The shares (value £15,000) are the traceable proceeds of the trust money. Beneficiaries claim beneficial ownership of the shares and capture the £5,000 gain. T's dissipation is treated as having come from T's own money.

Example 4 – Wrongful Mixture: Cherry-Picking (Shalson v Russo)

Facts

T is trustee of a trust. T mixes £20,000 of trust money and £20,000 of T's own money in one account (balance: £40,000).

T withdraws £20,000 and buys Company A shares (now worth £40,000 — doubled in value).

T withdraws the other £20,000 and buys Company B shares (now worth £10,000 — halved in value).

No money has been dissipated. T argues the trust money should be allocated to Company B (the less profitable use).

This is a two-party dispute: beneficiary vs trustee only.

Applying Shalson v Russo [2003] — Cherry-Picking

Since this is a two-party dispute (trustee vs beneficiary only), the beneficiary may attribute the MOST PROFITABLE application to the trust money.

Withdrawal

Current value

Attributed to

£20,000 (first withdrawal)

£40,000

£10,000

TRUSTEE's own money

Result: Beneficiaries claim beneficial ownership of Company A shares (worth £40,000) capturing the £20,000 gain. They would never choose Company B. Cherry-picking is NOT available where innocent third parties (e.g. creditors of a bankrupt trustee) would be prejudiced by it.

Example 5 – Innocent Mixture: Ratable Sharing (Non-Current Account)

Facts

T is trustee of both Trust A (£30,000) and Trust B (£10,000).

T wrongly mixes the two trust funds into one account (balance: £40,000).

T uses the entire £40,000 to purchase a portfolio of investments (now worth £60,000).

The mixture is INNOCENT from both trust beneficiaries' perspectives — T is the wrongdoer.

Applying Re Diplock — Ratable Sharing

Withdrawals from an innocent mixture are attributed rateably to contributors. All innocent parties must share rateably — one cannot be preferred over another (Foskett).

Contributor

Result: Each trust claims a proportionate share of the portfolio in proportion to its contribution. Both trusts capture their share of the appreciation. Neither can assert a lien over the other's share.

Example 6 – Innocent Mixture in a Current Account: All Three Methods Compared

Facts (from your exemplar notes)

The account begins with a zero balance.

Monday: T pays £10 from Trust A into the account.

Tuesday: T pays £10 from Trust B into the account.

Wednesday: T withdraws £10 and purchases a PAINTING.

Thursday: T pays £10 from Trust C into the account.

Friday: T withdraws £10 and purchases SHARES.

Remaining: £10 still credited to the account.

Question: How should the painting, the shares, and the remaining balance be attributed across Trusts A, B, and C?

Method 1 — Clayton's Case (FIFO: First In, First Out)

The first money paid in is deemed to be the first money withdrawn. Apply in strict chronological order of deposits.

Clayton's Case result: A traces into painting | B traces into shares | C holds the £10 balance. Note: the outcome depends entirely on the order of deposits — arguably arbitrary and potentially unfair. Readily displaced (Barlow Clowes).

Method 2 — Pari Passu Ex Post Facto (Single Calculation)

Identify total contributed by each trust as a fraction of the overall total. Apply that fraction to ALL withdrawals and the remaining balance in a single calculation regardless of timing.

Method 3 — Rolling Charge (Preferred)

Calculate fractions at the moment of EACH withdrawal based on who had contributed at that point. Recalculate after each new deposit.
