Which method applies? Start with FIFO (Clayton's Case). Displace it where result would be contrary to parties' intentions, impracticable, or unfair (Barlow Clowes). Where displaced, prefer rolling charge; use pari passu only if rolling charge is too difficult or expensive to apply.

Example 7 – Proprietary Claims in Practice: Choosing Between Options

Facts

T is trustee. T misapplies £50,000 of trust money, mixing it with £50,000 of T's own money (wrongful mixture, balance: £100,000).

T uses the entire £100,000 to buy land (now worth £140,000).

T is insolvent. T's unsecured creditors are owed £200,000.

The beneficiaries must decide what claim to make.

Analysis — Wrongful Mixture: Trust + Trustee's Own Money

Contributor

Amount

Fraction of land

Trust

£50,000

50/100 = 1/2 → £70,000 share of land

Trustee (own money)

£50,000

50/100 = 1/2 → £70,000 share of land

Total purchase price

£100,000

Land now worth £140,000

Option

Value Recovered

Why / When to Use

(A) Claim 1/2 proportionate share of land (£70,000)

£70,000 (captures £20k gain). Ranks as beneficial owner — AHEAD of T's unsecured creditors.

BEST option here: asset appreciated AND T is insolvent. Beneficiary is ringfenced from insolvency.

(B) Equitable lien for £50,000

£50,000 (secured). Ranks as secured creditor — still ahead of unsecured creditors.

Better than a personal claim if T insolvent, but does NOT capture appreciation. Worse than (A) here since asset has risen.

(C) Personal claim for breach of trust

£50,000 (unsecured). Ranks with unsecured creditors — worth perhaps 25p in the £ if T is insolvent.

WORST option if T is insolvent. Only viable if proprietary claim fails.

Critical point: T's interest in the land is SUBORDINATED to the beneficiaries' claim — T cannot assert their 1/2 share until the beneficiaries are fully satisfied. This further protects the beneficiaries in insolvency.

Flashcard Q&A

Question

Answer

What are the three steps in an equitable proprietary claim?

(1) Following – locate the asset; (2) Tracing – identify the substitute asset; (3) Claiming – assert personal or proprietary right.

What are the two Re Diplock conditions for equitable tracing?

(1) Claimant had equity-recognised right of property in the asset; (2) Asset held by a person in a fiduciary relationship with the claimant.

What is the rule in Re Hallett's Estate?

In a wrongful mixture, withdrawals/dissipations made for the trustee's own purposes are attributed to the TRUSTEE's money first, preserving trust money as the remaining balance.

How does Re Oatway modify Re Hallett?

Where an identifiable asset is purchased from a wrongful mixture and the remaining money is later dissipated, the identifiable asset is attributed to the TRUST. The order of withdrawals is not determinative.

When can a beneficiary cherry-pick the most profitable application?

Only where the dispute is SOLELY between the beneficiary and the trustee. Cannot cherry-pick where it would prejudice innocent third parties (Shalson v Russo).

What is Clayton's Case and when is it disapplied?

First in, first out rule for current accounts. Disapplied where result would be contrary to parties' intentions, impracticable, or unfair (Barlow Clowes). Disapplied in every post-Barlow Clowes case.

Which method is preferred when Clayton's Case is displaced?

Rolling charge method. Pari passu ex post facto is used only if rolling charge is too difficult or expensive to apply.

What claims are available for a WRONGFUL mixture (trust + trustee)?

(1) Proportionate share of the asset (captures gains); OR (2) Equitable lien for the amount misapplied. Trustee's interest is SUBORDINATED until beneficiary is fully satisfied.

What claims are available for an INNOCENT mixture?

Proportionate share ONLY. Cannot assert lien over innocent party's share. All innocent contributors share rateably.

When should a beneficiary choose an equitable lien over beneficial ownership?

Where the traced asset has DECREASED in value. The lien secures the full original sum as a priority claim, avoiding crystallising a loss by claiming a depreciated asset.

What is subrogation in the tracing context?

Where misapplied trust money discharges a secured debt, the beneficiary is subrogated to the creditor's security – treated as if they had loaned the money and holds equivalent security (Boscawen v Bajwa).

What is the principal defence to an equitable proprietary claim?

Bona fide purchaser for value of a LEGAL interest WITHOUT notice of the trust. Takes the property free of the equitable interest.

Common Exam Pitfalls

Pitfall

Correct Position

Confusing following with tracing

Following = tracking the SAME asset as it moves. Tracing = identifying a NEW SUBSTITUTE asset. They are different processes though often used together.

Applying Re Hallett to benefit the trustee

Hallett protects the BENEFICIARY, not the trustee. Re Oatway confirms the trustee CANNOT use Hallett to claim an identifiable asset purchased from a mixed fund when trust money was later dissipated.

Applying Clayton's Case automatically in current account scenarios

Clayton's Case has been disapplied in every case since Barlow Clowes where it would produce an unjust result. Always consider whether the result is fair before applying it.

Confusing wrongful and innocent mixture rules

WRONGFUL (trust + trustee): proportionate share OR lien; trustee's interest subordinated. INNOCENT (trust + innocent third party): proportionate share ONLY; cannot prefer one innocent party over another.

Always choosing beneficial ownership over the equitable lien

If the traced asset has FALLEN in value, claiming beneficial ownership crystallises a loss. The equitable lien is better — it secures the full original sum as a priority secured claim regardless of value movement.

Forgetting cherry-picking is only available in two-party disputes

Cherry-picking is available ONLY where the dispute is solely between the beneficiary and the trustee. It is unavailable where it would prejudice innocent third parties such as unsecured creditors of a bankrupt trustee (Shalson v Russo).

Omitting the bona fide purchaser defence

Always consider whether the person holding the traced asset is a bona fide purchaser of a LEGAL interest without notice. If so, the equitable proprietary claim fails against them — they take free of the equitable interest.

Confusing pari passu and rolling charge methods

Pari passu = single calculation using each contributor's fraction of the TOTAL paid in. Rolling charge = recalculate fractions at EACH withdrawal based on who had contributed up to that point. Rolling charge is preferred; pari passu is the fallback.

Formalities.title: W6: Formalities for Trusts of Land
Formalities.content:
Trusts Law – W6: Formalities for Trusts of Land

Topic Overview

1. s 53(1)(b) LPA 1925: declaration of trust of land (or interest in land) must be manifested and proved by signed writing

2. Key requirements: some writing; evidences settlor's intention and terms; signed by person able to declare the trust

3. Declaration and writing need not be contemporaneous; trust is unenforceable (not void) until formalities satisfied

4. Exceptions: s 53(2) LPA 1925 – resulting, constructive and statutory trusts exempt

5. Where legal title transferred but no s 53(1)(b) writing: court may enforce via constructive trust (Bannister v Bannister) or resulting trust (Hodgson v Marks)

6. Rochefoucald v Boustead: court will not allow statute to be used as an instrument of fraud

7. Failure of formalities in two-party situation: trustee denying trust vs settlor revoking

8. Three-party situation: trustee seeking to keep property vs settlor seeking return of property

Summary Revision Tables

Table 1: s 53(1)(b) LPA 1925 – Requirements in Detail

Requirement

Detail

The provision

"A declaration of a trust respecting any land or any interest therein must be manifested and proved by some writing signed by some person who is able to declare such trust or by his will."

"Manifested and proved"

Declaration and writing need NOT be contemporaneous. The trust can be validly created orally; it is simply unenforceable UNTIL s 53(1)(b) is satisfied. It does not need to have been in writing from the outset.

"Some writing"

No prescribed form. The writing must evidence: (1) the settlor's intention to create the trust; and (2) the terms of the trust.

"Signed by some person who is able to declare such trust"

Signature is usually the settlor's, but the TRUSTEE may also provide written evidence. Documents signed by an AGENT do NOT satisfy s 53(1)(b).

"By his will"

s 53(1)(b) expressly allows trusts of land to be created by will. A validly executed will (complying with s 9 Wills Act 1837) satisfies s 53(1)(b).

Effect of non-compliance

NON-COMPLIANCE renders the trust UNENFORCEABLE (not void). The settlor can change their mind; the trust will not become enforceable unless s 53(1)(b) is later satisfied or an exception applies.

Table 2: Exceptions to s 53(1)(b) – s 53(2) LPA 1925

Trust Type

Rule

Authority

Resulting trusts

Not subject to s 53(1)(b). Can arise over land without any written evidence.

s 53(2) LPA 1925; Hodgson v Marks [1971]

Constructive trusts

Not subject to s 53(1)(b). Can be imposed over land by the court without written evidence.

s 53(2) LPA 1925; Bannister v Bannister [1948]

Statutory trusts

Trusts arising under statute also exempt from s 53(1)(b) formalities.

s 53(2) LPA 1925

Table 3: Where Legal Title Transferred but No s 53(1)(b) Writing

Scenario / Case

Outcome

Basis

Rochefoucald v Boustead [1897]

Legal title transferred to defendant on oral understanding he would hold on trust. Defendant claimed statute as defence. Court ENFORCED the intended express trust despite absence of writing.

Equity will not allow s 53(1)(b) to be used as an instrument of fraud to deny the beneficiary's interest.

Bannister v Bannister [1948]

Vendor sold cottage at undervalue on oral understanding she could live there rent-free for life. Vendee later sought to evict her. Court imposed a CONSTRUCTIVE TRUST giving her a life interest.

Constructive trust; exempt from s 53(1)(b) under s 53(2). Unconscionable to deny interest.

Hodgson v Marks [1971]

Elderly widow transferred legal title to lodger on oral understanding she would retain beneficial ownership. Court recognised a RESULTING TRUST in her favour.

Resulting trust; exempt from s 53(1)(b) under s 53(2).

Table 4: Failure of Formalities – Consequences by Situation

Situation

Consequence

Gratuitous self-declaration; formalities never satisfied

Settlor can simply choose not to produce written evidence. In the absence of facts making it unconscionable to deny the trust, the beneficiary cannot assert any interest in the land.

Gratuitous transfer on trust; settlor changes mind; no constitution

Trust is BOTH unenforceable (for non-compliance with s 53(1)(b)) AND void (for lack of constitution). Subject to additional circumstances making denial unconscionable.

Legal title transferred; no s 53(1)(b) writing; trustee denies trust

Court may enforce via constructive trust (Bannister v Bannister) or resulting trust (Hodgson v Marks) – both exempt under s 53(2). Equity will not allow the statute to be used as an instrument of fraud (Rochefoucald).

Legal title transferred; no s 53(1)(b) writing; settlor seeks return

The trust may still be unenforceable. However, if the trustee has acted on the oral trust, the settlor's attempt to revoke may itself be unconscionable. Facts-specific.

Three-party: transfer on trust for third-party beneficiary; no writing

If trustee denies the trust → Rochefoucald / Bannister / Hodgson principles apply (constructive/resulting trust). If settlor seeks return → court will consider whether unconscionability prevents denial.

Table 5: s 53(1)(b) vs s 53(2) – Summary

Issue

s 53(1)(b)

s 53(2)

What it covers

Express trusts of land or any interest in land.

Resulting, constructive, and statutory trusts of land.

Formality required

Signed written evidence manifesting and proving the trust.

NONE – exempt from written formality.

Effect of non-compliance

Trust is UNENFORCEABLE (not void).

Trust can arise and be enforced without writing.

Agent's signature

Does NOT satisfy s 53(1)(b).

N/A

Flashcard Q&A

Question

Answer

What does s 53(1)(b) LPA 1925 require?

A declaration of a trust of land (or interest therein) must be MANIFESTED AND PROVED by some WRITING, SIGNED by a person able to declare such trust, or by will.

Does the writing need to be contemporaneous with the declaration?

NO. Declaration and writing need not be contemporaneous. The trust exists but is unenforceable until the formality is satisfied.

What is the consequence of failing to comply with s 53(1)(b)?

The trust is UNENFORCEABLE (not void). The settlor may change their mind if formalities are never satisfied.

Can an agent's signature satisfy s 53(1)(b)?

NO. Must be signed by the person able to declare the trust (usually the settlor) or the trustee. An agent's signature does not suffice.

What is the s 53(2) exception?

Resulting, constructive and statutory trusts of land are NOT subject to s 53(1)(b). They can arise without any written evidence.

What is the principle in Rochefoucald v Boustead?

Equity will not allow s 53(1)(b) to be used as an instrument of fraud. A court can enforce an intended trust even without the formalities where denial would be fraudulent.

What type of trust was imposed in Bannister v Bannister?

A constructive trust (exempt from s 53(1)(b) by s 53(2)), giving the vendor a life interest where she had sold at undervalue on the basis of an oral undertaking.

What type of trust arose in Hodgson v Marks?

A resulting trust (exempt under s 53(2)), where the widow retained beneficial ownership having transferred legal title on an oral understanding.

Common Exam Pitfalls

Pitfall

Correct Position

Saying non-compliance with s 53(1)(b) makes the trust void

NON-COMPLIANCE makes the trust UNENFORCEABLE, not void. The trust may still come to life if written evidence is later produced or if an exception applies.

Requiring contemporaneous writing

The writing need NOT be contemporaneous with the declaration. The trust can be created orally and evidenced in writing later.

Thinking an agent's signature satisfies s 53(1)(b)

An agent's signature does NOT satisfy s 53(1)(b). Only the person able to declare the trust (settlor or trustee) can sign.

Forgetting s 53(2) exemptions

Resulting and constructive trusts of land are EXEMPT from s 53(1)(b) under s 53(2). Always check whether the court can impose one of these to give effect to the parties' intentions.

Ignoring the fraud prevention principle

Where a person uses s 53(1)(b) as a cover for fraud (e.g. trustee denies trust after legal title transferred on oral understanding), equity will impose a constructive trust and prevent the statute operating as a fraud instrument (Rochefoucald; Bannister).

Purpose Trusts.title: W7: Purpose Trusts and Perpetuity
Purpose Trusts.content:
Trusts Law – W7: Purpose Trusts and Perpetuity

Topic Overview

1. Beneficiary principle: general rule – a trust must have a beneficiary to be enforceable

2. Charitable purpose trusts: exception – enforceable by A-G and Charity Commission; no perpetuity limit; cy-pres doctrine

3. Non-charitable purpose trusts: "trusts of imperfect obligation"; only valid within Endacott exceptions; common law perpetuity rules apply

4. Three requirements for charitable status: (1) charitable purpose (s 3(1) CA 2011); (2) public benefit (s 4); (3) wholly and exclusively charitable

5. Heads of charity under s 3(1) CA 2011: 13 heads including poverty, education, religion, health, community, arts, sport, human rights, environment, animal welfare, armed forces, and general analogous purposes

6. Public benefit: two elements – identifiable benefit + public or section of the public (Oppenheim test). No presumption of public benefit (s 4(2) CA 2011)

7. Wholly and exclusively charitable: mixed purposes – severance; incidental non-charitable purpose; charitable head covers it

8. Cy-pres doctrine: s 62 CA 2011 – five grounds for altering purpose. Initial vs subsequent failure; general charitable intention required for initial failure

9. Perpetuity rules: rule against remoteness of vesting (beneficiary trusts); rule against inalienability (non-charitable purpose trusts); 125-year statutory period

Summary Revision Tables

Table 1: Charitable vs Non-Charitable Purpose Trusts

Feature

Charitable Purpose Trust

Non-Charitable Purpose Trust

Enforceability

Legally enforceable. Enforced by Attorney-General; practical responsibility with Charity Commission.

Trusts of "imperfect obligation" – no recognised method of enforcement.

Validity

Valid if charitable purpose + public benefit + wholly and exclusively charitable.

Only valid if falls within a recognised Endacott exception (and only in wills).

Certainty of objects

Relaxed rules – sufficient to show intention to apply for a charitable purpose.

Void for uncertainty if purpose or means of achieving it is unclear (Re Astor).

Perpetuity

No limit on duration – can exist in perpetuity.

Subject to the common law rule against inalienability.

Failure of trust

Cy-pres doctrine – property applied to another charitable purpose.

Property results back to settlor on resulting trust.

Tax benefits

Significant tax benefits.

None.

Table 2: Requirements for Charitable Status

Requirement

Detail

(1) Charitable purpose

Must fall within s 3(1) CA 2011 (see Table 3 below) AND be for the public benefit. Both must be satisfied.

(2) Public benefit

No presumption of public benefit (s 4(2) CA 2011). Two elements: (a) IDENTIFIABLE BENEFIT – must be capable of identification; not settlor's belief; detriment is weighed against it. (b) PUBLIC OR SECTION OF PUBLIC – Oppenheim test: (i) beneficiaries not negligible in number; (ii) distinguishing quality does NOT depend on relationship to a particular individual.

(3) Wholly and exclusively charitable

If mixed charitable and non-charitable purposes: (a) INCIDENTAL/SUBSIDIARY non-charitable purpose → trust remains effective (Latimer v IRC). (b) SEVERABLE charitable and non-charitable portions → court severs if trust language permits and amounts are quantifiable (Salusbury v Denton; Re Coxen). (c) Otherwise: trust is void; property on resulting trust for settlor.

Table 3: Heads of Charitable Purpose (s 3(1) CA 2011) – Key Rules and Cases

Head

Key Rules

Cases

Prevention/Relief of Poverty

"Going short" (Re Coulthurst). Relative concept – judged against person's status in life. Does NOT mean destitution (Re Gardom). Cannot benefit the rich (Re Gwyon). Can be temporary. Can be inferred (Biscoe v Jackson). Public element relaxed for poverty trusts (Re Scarisbrick).

Re Coulthurst; Re Gardom; Re Young; Re Gwyon; Re Sanders WT

Advancement of Education

Not confined to formal instruction – includes training, research, broader education. Must involve element of teaching/passing knowledge into store of educational material (Re Hopkins). Increase in knowledge alone insufficient (Re Shaw). Research must be of educational value.

Re Gott; London Hospital Medical College v IRC; Re Shaw; Re Hopkins; Incorporated Council of Law Reporting v AG

Advancement of Religion

Includes belief in one or more gods or no god (s 3(2)(a)). Must ADVANCE the religion – positive steps to spread/sustain belief (United Grand Lodge). Does NOT cover cloistered communities with no public benefit (Gilmour v Coats). Public worship on land = charitable (Neville Estates).

R v Registrar General ex p Segerdal (Buddhism); Gilmour v Coats; Neville Estates v Madden

Advancement of Health / Saving Lives

Includes prevention/relief of sickness and promotion of health. CC takes broad view including complementary/holistic methods if sufficient evidence of benefit. Rescue services included.

Re Resch's Will Trust (hospitals); British Pregnancy Advisory Service

Citizenship / Community Development

Includes rural/urban regeneration and promotion of civic responsibility/volunteering. Focused on community not individual. Must be wholly and exclusively charitable.

IRC v Oldham TEC

Arts, Culture, Heritage or Science

Art must be "of merit" (Re Delius; Re Pinion – "junk" not charitable). Monuments of cultural/historical significance may qualify; monuments to private individuals unlikely. Heritage = history/traditions passed down through generations.

Re Delius; Re Pinion; Royal Choral Society v IRC; Williams Trustees v IRC

Advancement of Amateur Sport

s 3(2)(d): sport promoting health by involving physical or mental skill or exertion. Bridge: charitable (Hitchen Bridge Club – logical/lateral thinking). Target shooting: NOT charitable if fitness not GAINED from activity.

Hitchen Bridge Club; Cambridgeshire Target Shooting Association

Advancement of Human Rights

Must not stray into POLITICAL objectives (seeking to change the law). Raising awareness of human rights issues and securing enforcement of human rights law are acceptable.

McGovern v A-G (Amnesty – political; not charitable)

Environmental Protection

Includes preservation and conservation of natural environment and sustainable development. Independent expert advice may be needed to show subject is worthy of conservation.

—

Relief of Those in Need

Relief due to youth, age, ill-health, disability, financial hardship or other disadvantage. Does not require poverty; must show specific need (Joseph Rowntree).

Joseph Rowntree Memorial Trust Housing Association v A-G

Advancement of Animal Welfare

Includes welfare of particular types of animals (Re Moss – cats). Improving slaughtering methods (Re Wedgewood). NOT: care of particular named animals of testator. NOT: abolition of vivisection (political purpose) (National Anti-vivisection Society).

Re Moss; Re Wedgewood; National Anti-vivisection Society v IRC

Efficiency of Public Services

Armed forces, police, fire and rescue, ambulance services. Physical fitness (Re Gray); regiment library (Re Good).

Re Gray; Re Good

General/Analogous (s 3(1)(m))

Purposes recognised as charitable under previous law OR analogous to/within the spirit of earlier statutory heads and case law.

Catch-all provision

Table 4: Public Benefit – Key Rules

Issue

Rule

Authority

No presumption of benefit

s 4(2) CA 2011: there is no presumption of public benefit. Must always be positively established.

s 4(2) CA 2011

Identifiable benefit

Benefit must be capable of identification and description. Settlor's belief as to benefit is IRRELEVANT – question of fact. Benefit weighed against detriment (NAVS v IRC).

Re Hummeltenberg; NAVS v IRC

Public or section of public (Oppenheim test)

(1) Beneficiaries must not be negligible in number. (2) Distinguishing quality must NOT depend on relationship to a particular individual. Number alone does NOT guarantee public status.

Oppenheim v Tobacco Securities Trust [1951]; IRC v Baddeley

Poverty exception

For poverty trusts, the Oppenheim personal nexus requirement is RELAXED. Purpose must benefit a description of poor persons, not particular poor persons. "Poor relations" can be charitable (Re Scarisbrick; Dingle v Turner – employer).

Re Scarisbrick; Dingle v Turner

Charging for services

Charities may charge for services and even make a profit if reasonable/necessary to carry out aims. But opportunity to benefit cannot be unreasonably restricted by fees (ISC v Charity Commission).

Independent Schools Council v CC

Political objectives

Political objectives (seeking to procure changes to law/government policy) are NOT charitable. But political means may be used to achieve a non-political objective (e.g. environmental campaign for planning issues).

McGovern v A-G; CC Guidance

Table 5: Cy-pres Doctrine (s 62 CA 2011)

Issue

Rule

Doctrine

Where a charitable purpose trust fails, surplus funds are applied to another charitable purpose by way of a scheme established by the Charity Commission or the court. Prevents resulting trust back to settlor.

Subsequent failure

Once property has been given to charity, it is given for all time. Subsequent failure → cy-pres applies automatically without need to show general charitable intention.

Initial failure

Cy-pres only applies if the settlor showed a GENERAL CHARITABLE INTENTION (not merely an intention to benefit a particular object). Test: was the intention to benefit a particular object OR a general mode of charity?

Five statutory grounds (s 62 CA 2011)

(1) Original purpose fulfilled or cannot be carried out. (2) Purpose does not provide use for all the available property (surplus). (3) Property from similar trusts combined for more effective use. (4) Original purpose referred to an area or class of persons no longer relevant/suitable. (5) Purpose has been adequately provided for by other means; OR ceased to be charitable in law; OR ceased to provide a suitable and effective method of using the property.

Bisco v Jackson [1887] example

General charitable intention found: intended purpose (soup kitchen and cottage hospital in Shoreditch) was a specific object, but the underlying intention was to benefit the sick and poor of the parish. Cy-pres applied.

Flashcard Q&A

Question

Answer

What are the three requirements for charitable status?

(1) Charitable purpose falling within s 3(1) CA 2011; (2) Public benefit (s 4 CA 2011); (3) Wholly and exclusively charitable.

Is there a presumption of public benefit under CA 2011?

NO. s 4(2) CA 2011 expressly removes the presumption. Public benefit must always be positively established.

What is the Oppenheim test for public or section of public?

(1) Beneficiaries not negligible in number; (2) distinguishing quality does NOT depend on relationship to a particular individual.

In what circumstances is the Oppenheim test relaxed?

For POVERTY trusts – "poor relations" and employer-employee nexuses can be charitable. Poverty trusts are "of so altruistic a character" that the public element may be inferred.

What makes a purpose "political" and therefore non-charitable?

Seeking to PROCURE CHANGES to the law or government policy (McGovern v A-G). Using political means to achieve a non-political objective is permissible.

What is the cy-pres doctrine?

Where a charitable purpose fails, surplus funds are applied to another charitable purpose by way of a scheme (CC or court). Prevents a resulting trust to the settlor.

When is general charitable intention required for cy-pres?

Only for an INITIAL failure. Subsequent failure → cy-pres applies automatically. Initial failure → must show general charitable intention (intention to benefit a mode of charity, not a particular object).

What is the rule against inalienability?

Applies to non-charitable purpose trusts. The trust must not tie up property for a period exceeding the common law perpetuity period. Unlike the rule against remoteness, there is no statutory extension for these trusts.

What happened in Re Astor's Settlement Trusts?

A non-charitable purpose trust for "maintenance of good understanding between nations" and similar purposes was void: purposes were too inherently uncertain AND there was no mechanism of enforcement (no Endacott exception).

What is advancement of education – what does it require beyond mere increase of knowledge?

An element of teaching or education, or the result must pass into the store of educational material/improve communicable knowledge. A mere increase in knowledge without educational dissemination is insufficient (Re Shaw; Re Hopkins).

Common Exam Pitfalls

Pitfall

Correct Position

Assuming there is a presumption of public benefit

s 4(2) CA 2011 expressly REMOVES the presumption. Must always be shown positively.

Treating all advancement of education as charitable

Must be teaching/educational dissemination, not merely an increase in knowledge. Generating new knowledge without passing it on = insufficient (Re Shaw). Research must be of educational value (Re Hopkins).

Forgetting the relaxed public benefit test for poverty

The Oppenheim personal nexus test is RELAXED for poverty purposes. Poor relations and employer-employee connections can satisfy the public element (Re Scarisbrick; Dingle v Turner).

Assuming political activity = automatically non-charitable

Political MEANS to achieve a non-political objective are permissible. It is seeking to CHANGE THE LAW or government policy as a PRIMARY purpose that makes a trust non-charitable (McGovern v A-G; CC Guidance).

Applying cy-pres to initial failure without checking general charitable intention

Cy-pres is automatic only for SUBSEQUENT failure. For INITIAL failure (where the purpose never got started), must show GENERAL charitable intention (intention to benefit a mode of charity rather than a specific object).

Thinking non-charitable purpose trusts can be created inter vivos

Non-charitable purpose trust exceptions (Endacott categories) only apply where the trust is created IN A WILL. Cannot be created inter vivos.

Resulting Trusts.title: W8: Formalities, Constitution & Resulting Trusts
Resulting Trusts.content:
Trusts Law – W8: Formalities, Constitution and Resulting Trusts

Topic Overview

1. Three methods of disposition: outright gift; self-declaration of trust; transfer on trust

2. Constitution: valid transfer of legal title to trustee (or self-declaration = auto-constituted); method depends on asset type

Structure:

  • What was the disposor trying to do?
  • What formalities were necessary to perfect the transfer and did the disposor comply?
  • General rule: Equity does not perfect and imperfect gift or trust
  • What exceptions to the general rule are potentially applicable?
  • What are the requirements for the application of the exception(s) and were they satisfied?
  • Conclusion?

3. Milroy v Lord: equity will not perfect an imperfect gift. Transferor must do "everything necessary"; cannot use another method if intended method fails

4. Exceptions to Milroy v Lord: (1) Re Rose principle; (2) Mascall v Mascall extension – beyond transferor's control; (3) Pennington v Waine – unconscionability; (4) Strong v Bird – fortuitous vesting; (5) Donatio mortis causa

5. Resulting trusts: automatic (failed transfer on trust) and presumed (gratuitous transfer; purchase money)

6. Presumption of advancement: displaces resulting trust in certain relationships (husband to wife; parent to child; in loco parentis)

7. Testamentary trusts: governed by s 9 Wills Act 1837; PRs obliged to give effect to valid gifts/trusts in will

Summary Revision Tables

Table 1: Formalities for Creation of Inter Vivos and Testamentary Trusts

Trust Type

Method

Declaration Formalities

Constitution Formalities

Effect of Non-compliance

Inter vivos – Self-declaration (non-land)

Self-declaration

NONE – no prescribed formality. May be created orally, in writing, or by conduct (Paul v Constance).

AUTO-CONSTITUTED – no transfer of legal title required. Settlor retains legal title and becomes trustee.

N/A – no formality to breach. Trust simply may not be enforceable if intention is not sufficiently clear.

Inter vivos – Self-declaration (land)

Self-declaration

s 53(1)(b) LPA 1925 – must be manifested and proved by signed writing. Declaration and writing need NOT be contemporaneous. Agent’s signature insufficient.

AUTO-CONSTITUTED – settlor retains legal title as trustee. No transfer required.

Trust UNENFORCEABLE (not void) until written evidence produced. Settlor may change mind.

Inter vivos – Transfer on trust (non-land)

Transfer on trust

NONE – no prescribed formality for declaration of trust of non-land property. May be created orally, in writing, or by conduct (Paul v Constance).

Legal title must be transferred to trustee using the CORRECT METHOD for that asset type (see Table 2). Equity will not perfect imperfect transfer (Milroy v Lord). Exceptions: Re Rose; Mascall; Pennington; Strong v Bird; DMC.

Trust VOID – incompletely constituted. Automatic resulting trust if legal title was transferred but express trust fails.

Inter vivos – Transfer on trust (land)

Transfer on trust

s 53(1)(b) LPA 1925 – must be manifested and proved by signed writing. Covers both creation of beneficiary’s equitable interest AND declaration of the trust itself.

Transfer by DEED (s 52(1) LPA 1925) and registration at Land Registry (s 27 LRA 2002). Legal title passes on registration. Exceptions to Milroy v Lord apply.

Declaration failure: trust UNENFORCEABLE (s 53(1)(b)). Constitution failure: trust VOID. Both failures: trust void and unenforceable.

Testamentary trust (any property)

Created by will

s 9 Wills Act 1837 – will must be: (1) in writing; (2) signed by testator (or someone in their presence and by their direction); (3) testator’s signature made or acknowledged in the presence of two or more witnesses present at the same time; (4) each witness attests and signs. Also satisfies s 53(1)(b) for trusts of land.

On testator’s death, legal title to the estate vests in the personal representatives (PRs). PRs are obliged to constitute the trust by transferring legal title to the named trustees (or retain it if they themselves are the trustees). Formalities and constitution rules do NOT need to be separately considered once a valid will exists.

Non-compliance with s 9 Wills Act 1837: will is VOID. All gifts and trusts in it fail entirely. No separate formality/constitution analysis needed once a valid will exists.

Table 2: Constitution – How Legal Title Transfers (by Asset Type)

Asset

Method of Transfer

When Legal Title Passes

Key Authority

Registered land

Deed (s 52(1) LPA 1925); registered with Land Registry (s 27 LRA 2002).

On registration at Land Registry.

LPA 1925; LRA 2002

Shares

Signed stock transfer form sent to company registrar (with share certificate or indemnity).

When transferee registered in company's register of members.

Milroy v Lord; Re Rose; Zeital v Kaye

Choses in action (debts/bank accounts)

Written transfer plus written notice to debtor or bank (s 136 LPA 1925).

On receipt of notice.

s 136 LPA 1925

Chattels (incl. physical cash)

Deed of gift; OR delivery with evidence of intention to transfer.

On valid delivery with intention, or execution of deed.

Re Cole [1964]

Cheques / bills of exchange

Endorsement by transferor (signing name on back) per Bills of Exchange Act 1882.

On valid endorsement.

Bills of Exchange Act 1882; Jones v Lock

Table 3: Rule in Milroy v Lord – Core Principles

Principle

Detail

The rule

"Equity will not assist a volunteer." If trust property is not vested in the trustees, the trust is incompletely constituted and void. If legal title has not passed to the donee, the gift is imperfect and the donor can change their mind.

Must do "everything necessary"

Transferor must do "everything necessary to be done" to effect the intended disposition – i.e. follow the correct method for transferring legal title. If they fail, equity will NOT perfect the disposition or treat the transferor as having used one of the other methods.

No substitution of methods

If a donor intends to make a GIFT but fails to transfer legal title, they will NOT be treated as having self-declared a trust (Jones v Lock; Richards v Delbridge). They did not intend to assume the obligations of trusteeship.

Milroy v Lord facts

Settlor intended to transfer shares to Lord on trust. Completed a deed but did not complete the stock transfer form/register Lord. Held: trust not constituted. Equity would not treat settlor as having self-declared.

Table 4: Exceptions to Milroy v Lord

Exception

Conditions / Principle

Authority

Re Rose principle

Equitable interest passes to transferee (and transferor holds legal title on constructive trust) once transferor has done "everything in their power" to vest legal title. Conditions: (1) correct method of transfer used; (2) transferor has done everything in their power; (3) documentation in hands of person capable of effecting legal transfer. Disposition becomes irrevocable.

Re Rose [1952]; Milroy v Lord

Mascall v Mascall extension: matter beyond transferor's control

Transfer irrevocable if the transferor puts the matter BEYOND THEIR OWN CONTROL, even if documents not yet sent to person who can effect legal transfer. Sufficient that documents given to intended transferee. Contrast: sending to own agent/solicitor = insufficient (agency revocable). Key limit: Re Fry – where consent of Treasury required but not obtained before death, transfer ineffective.

Mascall v Mascall [1985]; Re Fry [1948]; Zeital v Kaye [2010]

Pennington v Waine: unconscionability

Equity will perfect an imperfect gift in any situation where it would be UNCONSCIONABLE for the donor to resile, even if Re Rose / Mascall conditions not strictly met. Documents need not have been sent off or delivered to donee. Goes further than Re Rose.

Pennington v Waine [2002]

Strong v Bird: fortuitous vesting

An imperfect gift or release is perfected if the intended donee obtains legal title in ANOTHER CAPACITY (usually as executor/administrator of donor's estate). Conditions: (1) intention to make an IMMEDIATE gift (Re Freeland – "an intention of giving, not an intention to give"); (2) intention continues UNCHANGED until donor's death (Re Gonin); (3) intended donee becomes EXECUTOR (Re Stewart – or one of several) or administrator (Re James). Equity will not enforce the debt/claim against the donee provided continuing intention shown.

Strong v Bird [1874]; Re Stewart [1908]; Re Freeland [1952]; Re Gonin [1979]; Re James [1935]

Donatio mortis causa (DMC)

Conditional gift made in contemplation of death. Conditions (Cain v Moon [1896]): (1) Gift made in CONTEMPLATION OF DEATH from an identifiable imminent cause (King v Dubrey); (2) Gift is CONDITIONAL on death (revocable if donor recovers); (3) Donor DELIVERS or parts with DOMINION over the property (can be constructive – Sen v Headley; Rahman v Hassan [2024]). Effect: donee may require PRs to complete legal title transfer after death. Full transfer of legal title not required before death.

Cain v Moon [1896]; Sen v Headley [1991]; King v Dubrey [2015]; Rahman v Hassan [2024]

Table 5: Resulting Trusts – Types and Rules

Type

How It Arises / Conditions

Effect / Rebuttal

Automatic RT: failed transfer on trust

Transfer on trust made to trustee but express trust wholly/partially fails (e.g. uncertainty of objects/subject matter; non-compliance with beneficiary principle; subsequent purpose failure). Legal title must have been transferred. If unconstituted: settlor retains property; nothing to result.

Beneficial interest automatically results back to settlor. Settlor gains S v V rights. No rebuttal available.

Automatic RT: partial failure or surplus

Validly created trust subsequently fails in part (e.g. runs full 125-year perpetuity period with property unvested and no gift-over; non-charitable purpose trust with surplus and no gift-over).

Undisposed-of beneficial interest results back to settlor (or estate). Arises automatically.

Presumed RT: gratuitous transfer

A transfers legal title of asset to B gratuitously (B provides no consideration) and no evidence A intended a gift.

B holds on resulting trust for A. Rebutted by evidence A intended a gift; if rebutted B becomes full owner.

Presumed RT: purchase money (full price)

A pays full purchase price for asset registered in B's name; B provides no consideration.

B holds on resulting trust for A unless shown A intended a gift or B provided consideration.

Presumed RT: purchase money (contribution)

A and B both contribute to purchase price; B is sole legal owner or proportions of legal ownership differ from contributions.

A and B hold equitable interests proportionate to their respective contributions, regardless of legal title. Rebutted by contrary common intention or express declaration of trust.

Presumption of advancement: husband to wife

Transfer from husband to wife (including during engagement). NOT from wife to husband or fiance to fiancee.

Displaces RT presumption. Transfer presumed to be an outright gift. Can be rebutted by contrary intention.

Presumption of advancement: parent to child

Transfer from parent to child (including adult child; less clear for mother). Also in loco parentis (person with financial responsibility for child; typically only while child remains minor).

Displaces RT presumption. Transfer presumed to be an outright gift. Can be rebutted by contrary intention.

Table 6: Issue-Spotting Guide – Formalities and Constitution

Step

Question to Ask

1. Identify the intention

Is this a GIFT, a SELF-DECLARATION OF TRUST, or a TRANSFER ON TRUST? The method used and formalities required differ accordingly.

2. Formalities for declaration

Is the subject matter LAND? If yes → s 53(1)(b) LPA 1925 applies; must be manifested and proved in signed writing. If non-land → no prescribed formality; can be oral, written, or by conduct (Paul v Constance).

3. Constitution (for gift or transfer on trust)

Has legal title been transferred to the donee/trustee using the CORRECT METHOD for that asset type? Self-declarations are auto-constituted (no transfer needed).

4. If constitution has failed

Consider the four exceptions to Milroy v Lord: (1) Re Rose; (2) Mascall / beyond control; (3) Pennington unconscionability; (4) Strong v Bird / DMC.

5. If transfer on trust fails

Was legal title actually transferred? If yes → automatic resulting trust arises. If not → settlor retains property (nothing to result back).

6. Gift to wrong method / precatory words

If intended as a gift but failed: equity will NOT treat as self-declaration (Jones v Lock; Richards v Delbridge). Must show clear evidence of intention to be a trustee.

Flashcard Q&A

Question

Answer

What is constitution of a trust?

The valid transfer of legal title from the settlor to the trustee (for a transfer on trust). Self-declarations are automatically constituted. Gifts: constitution = transfer to donee.

What is the rule in Milroy v Lord?

Equity will not perfect an imperfect gift or trust. Transferor must do "everything necessary" using the correct method. If they fail, equity will not perfect the disposition or treat them as having used another method.

What is the Re Rose principle?

If the transferor has done "everything in their power" to vest legal title, and used the correct method, equity treats the equitable interest as having passed. Transferor holds legal title on constructive trust pending registration.

How does Mascall v Mascall extend Re Rose?

Transfer is irrevocable if the transferor puts the matter "beyond their own control" – e.g. by handing documents to the intended transferee. Does not need to have been sent to the registrar.

What is the Pennington v Waine exception?

Equity will perfect an imperfect gift wherever it would be unconscionable for the donor to resile from it, even if the Re Rose conditions are not strictly met.

What are the three conditions for Strong v Bird to apply?

(1) Intention to make an IMMEDIATE gift; (2) Intention continues UNCHANGED until donor's death; (3) Intended donee becomes executor (or administrator – Re James) of donor's estate.

What are the three conditions for a valid donatio mortis causa?

(1) Gift in contemplation of IMMINENT death from an identifiable cause; (2) Conditional on death (revocable if donor recovers); (3) Donor delivers or parts with dominion over the property.

What is an automatic resulting trust?

Arises where a transfer on trust is made but the express trust fails. Beneficial interest automatically results back to the settlor (or their estate). No rebuttal available.

What is a presumed resulting trust and how is it rebutted?

Arises by presumption where property is gratuitously transferred or purchase money paid without consideration. Rebutted by evidence that the transferor actually intended to make a gift.

What is the presumption of advancement?

In certain relationships (husband to wife; parent to child; in loco parentis), a gratuitous transfer is presumed to be an outright gift, displacing the presumption of resulting trust. Can itself be rebutted by contrary intention.

Can a failed gift be treated as a self-declaration of trust?

NO (Milroy v Lord; Jones v Lock; Richards v Delbridge). The donor did not intend to take on the obligations of trusteeship and will not be treated as having done so.

What is the effect of an incomplete constitution on the trust?

The trust is void – trust property is not vested in the trustee so there is no trust. Equity will not compel the settlor to complete the transfer (equity will not assist a volunteer).

Common Exam Pitfalls

Pitfall

Correct Position

Treating a failed gift as a self-declaration of trust

Milroy v Lord prevents this. A donor who fails to gift property is NOT treated as a trustee. They did not intend to take on the onerous obligations of trusteeship.

Confusing Re Rose and Pennington v Waine

Re Rose requires the correct method AND doing everything within the transferor's power. Pennington goes further: sufficient that it would be unconscionable to withdraw, even without meeting Re Rose conditions strictly.

Confusing Strong v Bird with donatio mortis causa

Strong v Bird: donee becomes executor; gift perfected on testator's death. DMC: gift conditional on death; delivery of dominion while alive; does not require donee to be executor.

Forgetting that a future intention does not satisfy Strong v Bird

"An intention of giving, not an intention to give" is required (Re Freeland). A promise to give in the future does not satisfy Strong v Bird.

Applying presumption of advancement to wife → husband transfers

Presumption of advancement does NOT apply from wife to husband, only from HUSBAND to WIFE (and parent to child; in loco parentis to minor child).

Confusing automatic and presumed resulting trusts

AUTOMATIC: no rebuttal; arises automatically when transfer on trust fails. PRESUMED: arises by presumption in gratuitous transfer/purchase money scenarios; CAN be rebutted by evidence of gift intention.

Failing to check which asset type is involved for constitution

The correct method for constituting varies by asset. Using the wrong method = incomplete constitution and trust is void. E.g. simply handing over share certificates ≠ valid transfer of legal title in shares (Milroy v Lord).
